stem_paraphrase: An 8-month-old previously healthy infant is brought to the urgent care clinic with three days of vomiting and watery diarrhea. The infant attends a day care facility where several other children have had similar symptoms recently. His mother reports that he has become listless and has refused feedings. On examination he appears tired with tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, and decreased skin turgor. The abdomen is nontender with hyperactive bowel sounds. Compared with this infant's pre-illness baseline laboratory values, which set of findings is most likely present now?
options_paraphrase: A. Hematocrit decreased, serum albumin decreased, serum uric acid decreased
B. Hematocrit decreased, serum albumin decreased, serum uric acid normal
C. Hematocrit decreased, serum albumin increased, serum uric acid decreased
D. Hematocrit increased, serum albumin decreased, serum uric acid normal
E. Hematocrit increased, serum albumin increased, serum uric acid increased
F. Hematocrit increased, serum albumin normal, serum uric acid normal
explanation_gist: Acute gastroenteritis in an infant produces rapid loss of fluid through vomiting and diarrhea, depleting intravascular and extracellular volume. Three integrated lab findings follow. First, hemoconcentration: as plasma volume falls but red cell mass stays the same, the proportion of blood occupied by cells (hematocrit) rises. Second, serum proteins including albumin become concentrated in the smaller plasma volume, raising measured albumin levels even though no new protein has been made. Third, volume depletion reduces renal perfusion and lowers glomerular filtration rate. Since uric acid is normally cleared by glomerular filtration and proximal tubular handling that is sensitive to volume status (in volume contraction, more sodium and uric acid get reabsorbed in the proximal tubule), serum uric acid rises. The triad of elevated hematocrit, elevated albumin, and elevated uric acid is therefore a fingerprint of pure dehydration. Treatment is fluid replacement, which restores all three values toward baseline.

distractor_notes.A, B, C: Decreased hematocrit would indicate dilution (fluid overload, transfusion, anemia), not dehydration
distractor_notes.D: Albumin moves WITH hematocrit in dehydration (both concentrate together); they cannot diverge
distractor_notes.F: Hematocrit and albumin rise together in dehydration; uric acid also rises from reduced renal clearance

source_note: Original screenshot batch (no UWorld explanation captured); concept verified against standard physiology of acute dehydration.

stem_paraphrase: A 43-year-old man is evaluated for occasional retrosternal chest pressure that develops with moderate exertion and sometimes occurs at rest. He does not use alcohol, tobacco, or recreational drugs. There is an extensive family history of coronary artery disease. Temperature is 36.7 C, blood pressure 124/72 mm Hg, pulse 81 per minute, and respirations 14 per minute. Physical examination is unremarkable. Coronary angiography shows mild luminal irregularities but no significant obstructive lesions. During the procedure, infusion of acetylcholine into the coronary circulation produces dilation of the epicardial coronary vessels. A reaction involving which of the following amino acids is most likely responsible for the observed dilation?
explanation_gist: In a healthy coronary endothelium, acetylcholine binds endothelial muscarinic (M3) receptors and activates endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS). eNOS catalyzes the conversion of L-arginine and molecular oxygen into nitric oxide (NO) and L-citrulline. NO diffuses across the basement membrane into the underlying vascular smooth muscle, where it activates soluble guanylate cyclase. The resulting rise in cyclic GMP triggers smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation. The dilation observed with acetylcholine infusion therefore requires intact endothelium plus available L-arginine substrate. This question highlights a clinical paradox: in patients with significant atherosclerotic disease and dysfunctional endothelium, acetylcholine produces vasoCONSTRICTION instead of vasodilation, because the damaged endothelium fails to generate NO and the direct muscarinic effect on vascular smooth muscle (which is constrictive in the absence of NO release) predominates. The fact that this patient's vessels still dilated indicates relatively preserved endothelial function despite the family history. The same eNOS-NO pathway underlies endothelium-dependent flow-mediated dilation tested in research vascular studies. Other vasodilator pathways include adenosine (acting via A2 receptors on smooth muscle), prostacyclin (PGI2 from cyclooxygenase activity in endothelium), and shear-stress-induced NO release.

distractor_notes.B: Aspartate is a non-essential amino acid involved in the urea and aspartate-malate shuttle cycles, no direct role in vasodilation
distractor_notes.C: Glutamate is a major CNS excitatory neurotransmitter and TCA cycle intermediate, not a substrate for endothelial vasodilator synthesis
distractor_notes.D: Tryptophan is the precursor for serotonin and niacin synthesis, not vasodilation
distractor_notes.E: Tyrosine is the precursor for catecholamines (dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine, all VASOCONSTRICTORS), not for endothelial vasodilators

stem_paraphrase: A 32-year-old woman comes to the office for a preconception medical evaluation. She feels well, reports no health issues, and has regular menstrual cycles. The patient has been an elite long-distance swimmer since high school and recently retired from professional competition. She continues to practice swimming regularly. She does not use tobacco, alcohol, or recreational drugs. Blood pressure is 114/66 mm Hg and pulse is 60 per minute. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Compared with a person of similar age and sex with a sedentary lifestyle, which of the following sets of cardiac changes is most likely present in this patient (LV ejection fraction, LV cavity size, maximum cardiac output)?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased EF, increased LV cavity size, increased max CO
B. Increased EF, decreased LV cavity size, increased max CO
C. Increased EF, decreased LV cavity size, unchanged max CO
D. Unchanged EF, increased LV cavity size, increased max CO
E. Decreased EF, unchanged LV cavity size, unchanged max CO
explanation_gist: Endurance training imposes chronic volume overload on the heart because cardiac output must remain elevated for prolonged exercise sessions. The myocardium adapts via eccentric hypertrophy: new sarcomeres are added in SERIES (end to end) rather than in parallel, lengthening individual myocardial fibers and enlarging the LV chamber dimension. Stroke volume rises substantially because the larger ventricle can accept and eject more blood per beat. Resting heart rate falls (the marked bradycardia of trained endurance athletes, here 60 per minute) because the increased stroke volume meets the same resting cardiac output demand with fewer beats. EF (= SV / EDV) stays in the normal range or rises slightly because both numerator and denominator grow proportionally. Maximum cardiac output during peak exercise is substantially increased compared with a sedentary peer (often 35 to 40 L/min in elite endurance athletes versus 20 to 25 L/min in untrained controls), because peak SV is dramatically higher even though peak HR is similar. This contrasts with concentric hypertrophy seen with chronic pressure overload (sustained hypertension, aortic stenosis, resistance training), where sarcomeres are added in PARALLEL, the wall thickens without chamber enlargement, EF stays normal or rises modestly, but chamber size is unchanged or decreased.

distractor_notes.A: Decreased EF would suggest dilated cardiomyopathy, not the healthy adaptive eccentric hypertrophy of endurance training
distractor_notes.B, C: Decreased LV cavity size is the pattern of concentric hypertrophy from pressure overload (chronic HTN, AS), not endurance training
distractor_notes.E: Decreased EF and unchanged cavity size do not fit any healthy or trained cardiac adaptation

stem_paraphrase: A 48-year-old man with diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus is being evaluated for occasional retrosternal chest pain. He has no history of hypertension, and his blood pressure measurements during office visits have always been within normal limits. There is no family history of cardiovascular disease. The patient undergoes exercise treadmill stress testing. He walks for 7 minutes on the treadmill and stops due to fatigue without experiencing chest pain. His ECG does not show any abnormal changes. Heart rate ranges from 70 per minute at rest to 132 per minute at peak, and mean blood pressure ranges from 95 mm Hg at rest to 112 mm Hg at peak. Which of the following parameters was most likely decreased during peak stress compared with the resting state in this patient?
explanation_gist: During exercise, working skeletal muscle releases local metabolic vasodilators (adenosine, lactate, K+, CO2, H+) that produce profound arteriolar dilation in the active muscle beds. This regional vasodilation drops the resistance in the largest vascular bed of the body. Even though sympathetic activation produces compensatory vasoconstriction in non-exercising beds (splanchnic, renal), the net effect is a substantial decrease in total systemic vascular resistance. Despite the SVR drop, mean arterial pressure rises modestly during exercise (here 95 to 112) because cardiac output increases dramatically (HR roughly doubles to 132, and stroke volume also rises). The relationship MAP = CO x SVR holds: a large CO increase overpowers the SVR fall, raising MAP. Pulmonary artery systolic pressure rises with the increased CO crossing the pulmonary bed. Systemic systolic pressure also rises. LVEDP typically stays close to baseline or rises slightly with increased venous return. So the only parameter that drops during peak exercise is total SVR.

distractor_notes.A: Cardiac output rises substantially during exercise (HR x SV both increase)
distractor_notes.B: LVEDP is roughly unchanged at submaximal exercise, may rise slightly at peak
distractor_notes.C: Pulmonary artery systolic pressure rises modestly to accommodate the higher CO crossing the lung
distractor_notes.D: Systemic systolic BP rises during exercise, consistent with the MAP increase seen here

stem_paraphrase: A 54-year-old man is being evaluated for exertional dyspnea that started 6 months ago. The patient has no associated cough or wheezing. There is no known history of heart disease in his family. ECG, chest x-ray, exercise stress test, and echocardiogram are obtained to evaluate his symptoms. He subsequently undergoes right and left cardiac catheterization with the following results: arterial blood oxygen content 20 mL O2 per 100 mL blood; pulmonary artery diastolic pressure 12 mm Hg; left ventricular diastolic pressure 10 mm Hg; mean right atrial pressure 4 mm Hg; venous blood oxygen content 15 mL O2 per 100 mL blood. What additional information is necessary to calculate cardiac output in this patient?
options_paraphrase: A. Arteriovenous difference in oxygen content
explanation_gist: The Fick principle is the foundational method for calculating cardiac output from gas exchange data. It states that the rate at which a tissue takes up a substance equals blood flow through that tissue multiplied by the arteriovenous concentration difference of that substance. Applied to the lungs and using oxygen as the substance, cardiac output equals oxygen consumption divided by the arteriovenous oxygen content difference: CO = VO2 / (CaO2 - CvO2). Given the cath data: arterial oxygen content (CaO2) is 20 mL/100 mL, mixed venous oxygen content (CvO2) is 15 mL/100 mL, so the AV difference is already calculable as 5 mL/100 mL. The only missing parameter is VO2 (rate of oxygen consumption, typically measured by a metabolic cart or a Douglas bag). Once VO2 is obtained, CO follows directly. The other parameters listed are either already derivable from the given data (oxygen-binding capacity and Hb saturation underlie the oxygen content values), already provided, or irrelevant (respiratory quotient is for CO2/O2 metabolism ratios, not directly used in Fick CO calculation).

distractor_notes.A: AV difference in oxygen content is already calculable from the given CaO2 (20) and CvO2 (15) values: 20 - 15 = 5 mL/100 mL
distractor_notes.B: Oxygen binding capacity of Hb is incorporated into the oxygen content values already given, so not separately needed
distractor_notes.C: Hemoglobin saturation is also incorporated into the oxygen content values
distractor_notes.E: Respiratory quotient (CO2 produced / O2 consumed) is used in indirect calorimetry, not in the Fick equation for CO

stem_paraphrase: A 32-year-old woman is recruited for a research study. She is evaluated in the hospital as part of the study protocol. Her medical history includes an uncomplicated appendectomy, and her medications include oral contraceptive pills and a multivitamin. During the evaluation, increases in heart rate and cardiac output are observed, and an arterial blood gas study taken at the same time shows normal partial pressures of arterial oxygen (PaO2) and carbon dioxide (PaCO2). These changes are most likely part of an integrated response to which of the following?
explanation_gist: The hallmark of normal exercise physiology is matched scaling of cardiac output and minute ventilation to metabolic demand. During exercise, the working muscles drive massive increases in oxygen consumption and CO2 production. The cardiovascular response includes sympathetic activation that raises heart rate (chronotropy), stroke volume, and contractility (inotropy), producing a substantial rise in cardiac output (from ~5 L/min at rest up to 25 L/min or more in trained individuals at peak). Simultaneously, the respiratory system increases ventilation through neural and chemoreceptor feedback to perfectly match CO2 elimination to production and oxygen uptake to consumption. As a result, arterial PaO2 and PaCO2 remain essentially normal across the full range of submaximal to maximal exercise. This is what distinguishes exercise from the other listed conditions: each of them disrupts gas exchange in a characteristic way that would show on the ABG. Carotid sinus massage triggers a vagal response and BRADYCARDIA (opposite of increased HR). High altitude lowers PaO2 (the inspired partial pressure drops). Panic attack with hyperventilation drops PaCO2 (respiratory alkalosis). Pulmonary artery occlusion (e.g. PE) often produces hypoxemia from V/Q mismatch and hypocapnia from compensatory hyperventilation, plus signs of right heart strain.

distractor_notes.A: Carotid sinus massage triggers parasympathetic outflow and BRADYCARDIA, with falls in CO; the opposite of this patient's increased HR and CO
distractor_notes.C: High altitude lowers inspired PO2 and therefore lowers PaO2; compensatory hyperventilation also drops PaCO2. Both gases would deviate from normal
distractor_notes.D: Panic attack with hyperventilation drops PaCO2 (respiratory alkalosis); PaCO2 would not remain normal
distractor_notes.E: PA occlusion causes hypoxemia from V/Q mismatch and often hypocapnia from compensatory hyperventilation; gases would not stay normal

stem_paraphrase: A 64-year-old man presents to the office with chest discomfort and mild shortness of breath that started one hour ago after an argument with his wife. He has a long history of hypertension and diabetes mellitus. His blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg and his heart rate is 100 per minute. An EKG taken in the emergency department shows no signs of acute ischemia. Cardiac auscultation in the left decubitus position on full expiration reveals a presystolic sound. Which of the following best explains this physical examination finding?
options_paraphrase: E. Mitral leaflet fusion and fibrosis
explanation_gist: A presystolic heart sound, occurring just before S1, is the fourth heart sound (S4) or atrial gallop. S4 is generated when the atria contract forcefully to push blood into a ventricle that has become stiff and non-compliant. The atrial kick (active filling phase of diastole, contributing ~25% of LV filling in normal hearts) collides with the resistant LV wall, producing the audible low-frequency thump immediately before S1. The most common cause is concentric LV hypertrophy from chronic pressure overload: long-standing hypertension, severe aortic stenosis, or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The thickened muscular wall has reduced compliance, so any given filling volume produces a higher intracardiac pressure rise (steeper end-diastolic pressure-volume relationship). The S4 is best heard with the bell of the stethoscope at the cardiac apex, with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position to bring the LV apex closest to the chest wall, and on held expiration to reduce competing breath sounds. This patient's hypertension, diabetes, and current hypertensive episode (180/100) all support concentric LVH as the underlying cause. An S4 also occurs in acute MI as ischemic tissue becomes acutely stiff, and in active angina from transient diastolic dysfunction. In contrast, S3 is heard in EARLY diastole (during passive rapid filling), reflects LV cavity enlargement and reduced compliance to volume overload, and is common in dilated cardiomyopathy, severe MR, severe AR, and high-output states.

distractor_notes.A: Papillary muscle dysfunction acutely causes mitral regurgitation, producing a holosystolic murmur, not a presystolic sound
distractor_notes.C: Increased preload alone would produce an S3 (during rapid passive filling), not an S4. S4 reflects reduced LV compliance, not increased filling
distractor_notes.D: AR produces a diastolic decrescendo murmur, not a presystolic sound; though AR can secondarily cause LV dilation and an S3
distractor_notes.E: Mitral leaflet fusion is mitral stenosis, which produces an opening snap and diastolic rumble, not a presystolic sound (though severe MS may have a loud S1)

stem_paraphrase: A 44-year-old man with progressive dyspnea is diagnosed with dilated cardiomyopathy. Despite optimal medical therapy, he continues to have symptoms and disease progression is noted. He undergoes cardiac transplantation after a suitable donor becomes available. Permission is obtained from the patient to study his diseased heart for intracellular calcium regulation. Microelectrodes placed into cardiac muscle cells detect a rapid decrease in cytoplasmic calcium level immediately preceding relaxation. Which of the following proteins is most likely responsible for the observed change in electrolyte levels?
explanation_gist: During each cardiac cycle, cytoplasmic calcium rises to trigger contraction and must then fall to allow relaxation. The rise comes from two sources: a small influx through voltage-gated L-type calcium channels during phase 2 of the action potential, and a much larger release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum through ryanodine receptors (calcium-induced calcium release). To enable relaxation, this calcium must be cleared from the cytoplasm rapidly. Two main extrusion pathways do this: (1) SERCA (sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum Ca-ATPase) pumps calcium back into the SR using ATP, accounting for roughly 70 percent of calcium removal in a healthy ventricular myocyte. (2) The Na/Ca exchanger (NCX) in the sarcolemma extrudes calcium across the cell membrane to the extracellular space, using the inward sodium gradient as its energy source, accounting for roughly 30 percent of calcium removal. Among the listed options, only NCX actively moves calcium OUT of the cytoplasm. SERCA is not in the answer choices. The other proteins either bring calcium into the cytoplasm (RyR releases from SR; L-type channels bring in extracellular Ca) or bind calcium for signaling purposes (calmodulin, troponin C). In failing dilated cardiomyopathic hearts, SERCA expression and activity are typically reduced, so NCX takes on a relatively larger share of calcium clearance, and overall calcium handling is slower, contributing to impaired relaxation (lusitropic failure) and reduced contractility.

distractor_notes.A: Calmodulin binds calcium to enable downstream signaling (CaMK activation), but does not transport calcium across membranes
distractor_notes.C: Ryanodine receptors RELEASE calcium from SR into cytoplasm (raising cytoplasmic Ca), opposite of what is being asked
distractor_notes.D: Troponin C binds calcium to trigger thin filament conformational changes that enable cross-bridge cycling; it does not move calcium
distractor_notes.E: L-type calcium channels bring Ca INTO the cytoplasm during phase 2 of the AP, opposite direction; they are not extruders

stem_paraphrase: A large pharmaceutical company is conducting research on an experimental drug that affects blood flow in diseased tissue. A healthy 30-year-old man is enrolled in the trial, and patterns of normal blood flow through various tissues are studied. One of the patterns is shown: a tracing where blood flow drops sharply during a specific portion of the cardiac cycle (synchronized with the QRS / T wave time period of the ECG) and rises back during the rest of the cycle. The blood flow curve was most likely obtained from tissue of which of the following?
image_description: A graph plotting blood flow (y-axis) versus time (x-axis). The flow tracing has a characteristic biphasic pattern: a high baseline that drops abruptly to near zero during the systolic portion of the cardiac cycle (timed with the QRS complex on the ECG shown below), then quickly returns to baseline during diastole. The ECG trace is shown below the flow trace, with the systolic dip in flow aligned to the QRS-T window of the cardiac cycle.
explanation_gist: Coronary blood flow is unique in the body for being PHASIC, dropping markedly during systole and rising during diastole. The reason is mechanical: during systole, the contracting LV wall generates intramural pressure that approaches or even exceeds aortic systolic pressure. This high intramural pressure compresses the small intramyocardial coronary arterioles, occluding flow through them despite the high systemic pressure pushing forward. As the LV relaxes during diastole, intramyocardial pressure drops to near zero, the arterioles re-open, and flow surges (driven by aortic diastolic pressure that has been steadily falling but is still well above myocardial venous pressure). Roughly 80 percent of LV coronary blood flow occurs during diastole. This phasic pattern is most extreme in the LEFT coronary distribution because LV systolic pressure is much higher than RV systolic pressure (approximately 120 mm Hg vs 25 mm Hg). RV coronary flow shows much less systolic dip because RV systolic pressure (~25 mm Hg) only partially compresses RV coronary vessels. Brain, kidney, and other organs have continuous flow throughout the cardiac cycle with only minor pulse pressure variation; they do not show the systolic blackout pattern shown. Clinically, this explains why coronary perfusion pressure equals aortic diastolic pressure minus LV end-diastolic pressure, and why tachycardia (which shortens diastole more than systole) compromises coronary supply.

distractor_notes.A: Adrenal medulla has continuous arterial flow, no systolic occlusion pattern
distractor_notes.B: Brain cortex receives continuous flow modulated by autoregulation, not phasic systolic interruption
distractor_notes.D, E: Renal cortex and medulla have continuous flow modulated by autoregulation
distractor_notes.F: RV myocardium shows much less systolic dip because RV systolic pressure is only about 25 mm Hg, not enough to fully compress RV coronary vessels
distractor_notes.G: Subcortical brain nuclei have continuous flow

stem_paraphrase: A 54-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with cough, shortness of breath, and chest discomfort. He has a known history of coronary artery disease and underwent coronary artery bypass graft surgery 3 years ago. As a result of drug therapy, the left ventricular pressure-volume loop changes from a solid baseline outline to a dashed post-therapy outline (with the loop unchanged in regions where no dashed line is shown). Which of the following parameters is most likely increased as a result of this change?
image_description: A pressure-volume loop diagram comparing baseline (solid line) to post-therapy (dashed line). The dashed loop shows a higher peak ejection pressure (top of loop pushed upward) and a leftward shift of the end-systolic point (lower end-systolic volume), with the end-diastolic point relatively unchanged. The net result is that the dashed loop is wider horizontally (larger stroke volume) and taller (higher peak pressure), while the position of the end-diastolic point is essentially unchanged from baseline.
explanation_gist: The dashed PV loop shows increased contractility (positive inotropy), the classic signature being a leftward shift of the end-systolic point at unchanged end-diastolic volume, accompanied by a higher peak ejection pressure. Mechanically, increased contractility means the ventricle ejects more vigorously and ejects to a smaller residual volume during each cycle. Stroke volume equals end-diastolic volume minus end-systolic volume; with EDV unchanged but ESV smaller, SV must be larger. The new loop is therefore wider on the volume axis. The slope of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR) line, which connects the end-systolic points across various loading conditions, becomes STEEPER with positive inotropy and SHALLOWER with negative inotropy. Drugs producing this pattern include digoxin (in HFrEF), dobutamine, milrinone, and endogenous sympathetic surge. In this CABG patient with acute decompensation, the most likely drug is one of these positive inotropes. Note: the loop also shows higher peak ejection pressure because the more forceful contraction generates more pressure against the same afterload. EDP and compliance are unchanged (the diastolic limb is unaltered). Preload is unchanged (EDV is the same).

distractor_notes.A: End-diastolic pressure is unchanged (the bottom of the loop is at the same EDV and pressure)
distractor_notes.B: End-systolic VOLUME is DECREASED (shifted leftward) with positive inotropy, not increased
distractor_notes.D: Ventricular compliance (slope of the diastolic filling segment) is unchanged in the dashed loop
distractor_notes.E: Preload (EDV at the bottom of the loop) is unchanged

stem_paraphrase: A 34-year-old woman who recently moved to the United States from Russia presents with progressive weakness, shortness of breath on exertion, and difficulty breathing while lying flat. Cardiac auscultation at the apex reveals an opening snap followed by a low-pitched rumbling diastolic murmur. A labeled left ventricular pressure-volume loop is shown with six points marked A through F. Which of the six points corresponds most closely to the moment when the opening snap is generated?
image_description: Standard LV pressure-volume loop with six labeled points. E sits at the top of the loop (peak systolic pressure during ejection), C at the upper-left corner (aortic valve closure), B on the upper-right limb (aortic valve opening), A at the lower-right corner (mitral closure / end-diastole), D at the lower-left corner (mitral valve opening), F at the bottom middle (mid-filling phase).
explanation_gist: The patient has mitral stenosis from prior rheumatic fever, far more common in regions with poor access to antibiotic treatment for streptococcal pharyngitis. The opening snap is generated when the thickened, fused mitral leaflets are forced apart by left atrial pressure rising above LV pressure during early diastole. On a PV loop, this transition is the bottom-left corner where the descending isovolumetric relaxation segment pivots into the rightward-moving filling segment. The four cardinal PV loop transition points are: mitral closure (end-diastole, bottom-right), aortic opening (start of ejection, top-right), aortic closure (end-systole, top-left), and mitral opening (start of filling, bottom-left). Point D matches this last transition.

distractor_notes.A: End-diastole, where the mitral valve closes (this is when S1 is generated, not OS)
distractor_notes.B: Aortic valve opening, normally silent
distractor_notes.C: Aortic valve closure, which generates S2 not OS
distractor_notes.E: Peak ejection pressure during systole, silent
distractor_notes.F: A mid-filling location, not a valve event

stem_paraphrase: A 56-year-old man arrives in the emergency department reporting that his heart feels fast and irregular. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or lightheadedness. He typically drinks alcohol only a few times per year, but the previous night he hosted his wife's birthday party and consumed a large quantity of alcohol. He has no other medical history and takes no medications. Pulse examination confirms an irregularly irregular rhythm at 138 beats per minute. Which of the following ECG tracings would most closely match this patient's rhythm?
options_paraphrase: A. Ventricular bigeminy: a normal sinus beat alternating with a wide-complex premature ventricular beat in a predictable pattern
B. Sustained ventricular tachycardia: monomorphic wide QRS complexes at a regular rapid rate
C. Atrial flutter with fixed AV conduction: saw-tooth flutter waves with a regular ventricular response
D. Atrial fibrillation: chaotic fibrillatory baseline with no discernible P waves and irregularly irregular narrow QRS intervals
E. AV nodal reentrant tachycardia: regular narrow QRS rhythm with P waves buried in or just after the QRS
image_description: Five ECG strips, one per answer choice, illustrating each rhythm. The atrial fibrillation strip shows narrow QRS complexes spaced irregularly with no visible P waves and a chaotic baseline between beats.
explanation_gist: The scenario describes holiday heart syndrome: acute atrial fibrillation precipitated by binge alcohol intake. AF on ECG has three hallmark features: absent organized P waves replaced by chaotic fibrillatory undulations, irregularly irregular R-R intervals with no underlying repeating pattern, and a typically rapid uncontrolled ventricular response above 100 per minute. Mechanistically, foci of rapid atrial electrical activity (often originating near the pulmonary venous ostia) fire at 400 to 600 per minute. The AV node filters and transmits these impulses unpredictably to the ventricles. Common predisposing and triggering factors include age-related atrial remodeling, hypertension, valvular disease (especially mitral stenosis), hyperthyroidism, sympathetic surge from sepsis or stress, and excessive alcohol intake.

distractor_notes.A: Ventricular bigeminy is regularly irregular: every sinus beat is followed by a PVC at a predictable interval, distinguishable from the chaotic AF pattern
distractor_notes.B: VT shows wide QRS at a regular rapid rate, not narrow QRS irregularly spaced
distractor_notes.C: Atrial flutter with fixed conduction produces a regular ventricular response despite the flutter waves
distractor_notes.E: AVNRT is a regular tachycardia, not irregularly irregular

stem_paraphrase: A 65-year-old woman is brought to the hospital with sudden chest heaviness and shortness of breath that began one hour earlier. She has type 2 diabetes, obesity, and hypertension. Temperature is 36.7 C, blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg, pulse is 110 per minute, and respirations are 20 per minute. Her extremities are cold and clammy with notable diaphoresis. ECG demonstrates ST-segment elevation in the lateral leads. Which combination of cardiac contractility, systemic vascular resistance, and pulmonary artery systolic pressure is most likely present in this patient?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased contractility, decreased SVR, decreased PA systolic pressure
B. Decreased contractility, increased SVR, decreased PA systolic pressure
C. Decreased contractility, increased SVR, increased PA systolic pressure
D. Increased contractility, decreased SVR, decreased PA systolic pressure
E. Increased contractility, increased SVR, increased PA systolic pressure
explanation_gist: Acute lateral wall STEMI with hypotension and cold clammy extremities indicates cardiogenic shock from left ventricular pump failure. Three concurrent hemodynamic changes follow. First, infarcted myocardium contracts poorly, so cardiac contractility falls. Second, the resulting drop in blood pressure unloads the carotid and aortic baroreceptors, removing tonic inhibition of sympathetic outflow. The sympathetic surge produces intense peripheral arteriolar vasoconstriction, raising systemic vascular resistance markedly (this is what makes the extremities cold and clammy). Third, the failing LV cannot eject enough blood forward, so LV end-diastolic pressure climbs and transmits retrograde through the left atrium into the pulmonary venous system, raising pulmonary capillary wedge pressure. To continue moving blood forward across the now higher pulmonary venous pressure, pulmonary artery systolic pressure must also rise. If the RV cannot generate this higher pressure, right ventricular failure follows.

distractor_notes.A: Pattern fits warm distributive shock (sepsis) where SVR drops, not cardiogenic
distractor_notes.B: Hypovolemic shock pattern with low filling pressures and increased SVR, but contractility and PA pressure are typically preserved or low
distractor_notes.D: Increased contractility with this hypotension would not be expected
distractor_notes.E: Increased contractility does not fit pump failure

stem_paraphrase: A 45-year-old woman enrolled in a cardiovascular health study wears a continuous 24-hour ambulatory ECG monitor. Intermittent occurrences of a specific pattern were captured. Three beats are labeled: X is a normal sinus beat, Y is a wide-complex beat with a T wave deflected opposite to the QRS, and Z is the beat that follows a brief pause after Y. Which option best ranks the relative left ventricular end-diastolic volumes just prior to each of these three beats?
options_paraphrase: A. X = Y = Z (all three EDVs equal)
B. X > Y > Z (sinus beat highest, post-PVC beat lowest)
C. Y > X > Z (PVC has the highest EDV)
D. Y = Z > X (PVC and post-PVC both higher than sinus)
E. Z > X > Y (post-PVC beat highest, PVC lowest)
image_description: An ECG strip showing three beats labeled X, Y, and Z. X is a normal narrow-complex sinus beat. Y is a premature wide-complex beat with the T wave deflected in the direction opposite to the QRS, occurring before the next expected sinus beat. After Y there is a noticeably longer pause, followed by Z, a normal-appearing narrow-complex beat.
explanation_gist: Beat Y is a premature ventricular contraction, recognized on ECG by its wide QRS, oppositely-directed T wave, and the brief pause that follows. PVCs originate in ectopic ventricular foci with enhanced automaticity and depolarize the ventricles ahead of schedule, before the LV has finished its usual diastolic filling time. The EDV for beat Y is therefore reduced compared with a baseline sinus beat. The compensatory pause that follows occurs because the SA node continues firing on its own schedule, but the next sinus impulse arrives during the ventricular refractory period and is blocked. The next successfully conducted beat (Z) therefore has a longer diastolic filling interval than usual, producing a larger EDV. The forceful contraction generated by this larger preload is sometimes felt as a palpitation. The correct ranking is Z (post-PVC, longest fill time) greater than X (sinus, normal fill time) greater than Y (PVC, shortened fill time).

distractor_notes.A: Ignores that PVC timing reduces filling time and the compensatory pause extends it
distractor_notes.B: Reversed: Z should be highest, not lowest
distractor_notes.C: PVC has reduced filling, not the highest EDV
distractor_notes.D: Misses that the PVC has reduced EDV compared with the post-PVC beat

stem_paraphrase: A 44-year-old man is hospitalized after a motor vehicle collision with multiple injuries. A traumatic arteriovenous fistula has formed as a result of vascular trauma. Which of the following pressure-volume loop changes would most likely develop in his left ventricle as a result of this fistula? Each option shows a pair of loops where the solid line represents the preinjury state and the dashed line the postinjury state.
options_paraphrase: A. PV loop showing increased height with steeper end-systolic relationship and modest increase in stroke volume (positive inotrope pattern)
B. PV loop showing widened stroke volume with slightly lower peak pressure and leftward shift of end-systolic point (reduced afterload with increased preload)
C. PV loop showing higher peak pressure with reduced stroke volume (increased afterload pattern)
D. PV loop showing narrowed loop shifted leftward with reduced filling (decreased preload pattern)
image_description: Four labeled PV loop diagrams (A through D), each comparing a solid preinjury baseline loop with a dashed postinjury loop. Option B shows the dashed loop wider horizontally than the baseline with end-diastolic volume shifted slightly rightward (more filling) and end-systolic volume shifted leftward (more emptying), with a marginally lower peak pressure.
explanation_gist: An arteriovenous fistula creates a low-resistance shortcut from the arterial side directly to the venous side, bypassing the high-resistance arteriolar bed. Three hemodynamic consequences follow. First, the parallel low-resistance pathway lowers overall systemic vascular resistance, so the LV ejects against less afterload. Second, blood returns rapidly to the right heart through the venous limb, raising venous return and increasing LV preload (the EDV grows). Third, the combination of less afterload and more preload increases stroke volume, widening the PV loop horizontally and producing a slightly lower peak ejection pressure. Baseline contractility is initially unchanged (the slope of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship is preserved), although a sustained reflex may eventually adjust it. Large chronic AV fistulas can drive high-output heart failure if the LV cannot keep pace with the elevated venous return demand.

distractor_notes.A: Steeper ESPVR with increased contractility describes a positive inotrope (such as digoxin), not the AV fistula effect
distractor_notes.C: Higher afterload with reduced SV fits aortic stenosis or systemic vasoconstrictor, opposite of the AV fistula physiology
distractor_notes.D: Reduced preload pattern matches a venodilator like nitroglycerin, not increased venous return from a fistula

stem_paraphrase: A 4-week-old boy is scheduled for cardiac catheterization to close a patent ductus arteriosus. He was born at 26 weeks gestation following a placental abruption and has been in the neonatal intensive care unit since delivery. Pulse oximetry shows 99% saturation in both the right hand and the left foot. Echocardiography demonstrates a moderately sized PDA. Blood gases are sampled from several intracardiac and great-vessel locations before closure. Compared with a healthy newborn without a PDA, in which of the following locations would this patient's partial pressure of oxygen most likely be higher than normal?
explanation_gist: After birth, pulmonary vascular resistance normally falls and aortic pressure exceeds pulmonary arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. When the ductus arteriosus fails to close (as is common in premature infants), this pressure gradient drives oxygenated blood from the descending aorta backward through the PDA into the main pulmonary artery, both during systole and diastole. The deoxygenated blood that the right ventricle normally ejects into the pulmonary artery (PaO2 around 40 mm Hg) gets mixed with this incoming oxygenated aortic blood, raising the measured PaO2 of pulmonary artery samples above the normal value. All other compartments retain their usual oxygen content: the aorta receives normally oxygenated blood from the LV, the left atrium continues to receive blood from the pulmonary veins (which is fully oxygenated regardless of shunt presence), and the right atrium receives systemic venous return that is upstream of the PDA shunt point. If the shunt later reverses (Eisenmenger physiology with elevated pulmonary pressures), aortic PaO2 could drop and the patient would become cyanotic, which is not described here.

distractor_notes.A: The ascending aorta is upstream of the PDA shunt; its blood is normally oxygenated by the LV and is not affected
distractor_notes.B: Descending aortic blood proximal to the shunt is also fully oxygenated, unchanged from normal
distractor_notes.C: Left atrial blood comes from the pulmonary veins; flow volume is higher with left-to-right shunting, but the oxygen content per unit is unchanged
distractor_notes.E: Right atrial blood is systemic venous return, upstream of where the PDA delivers oxygenated blood, so its PaO2 is unchanged

stem_paraphrase: A 32-year-old man is evaluated in the emergency department for fever, night sweats, and chills lasting several days. He recently used intravenous drugs and was previously healthy. Temperature is 38.3 C, blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg, and pulse is 105 per minute and regular. Further workup demonstrates aortic valve endocarditis with an intracardiac abscess and a small fistula between the right ventricle and the aortic root that remains patent throughout the cardiac cycle. Doppler ultrasonography of the fistula is most likely to show which of the following flow patterns?
options_paraphrase: A. Continuous flow from the aortic root into the right ventricle across the full cardiac cycle
B. Flow from the aortic root into the right ventricle only during diastole
C. Flow from the aortic root into the right ventricle only during systole
D. Continuous flow from the right ventricle into the aortic root across the full cardiac cycle
E. Flow from the right ventricle into the aortic root only during diastole
F. Flow from the right ventricle into the aortic root only during systole
image_description: Cross-sectional cardiac diagram showing aortic root pressure of 120/80 mm Hg, pulmonary artery 25/10 mm Hg, left atrium pressure under 10, right atrium pressure under 5, and left ventricle 120/10. A small communication is drawn between the aortic root and the right ventricle.
explanation_gist: Aortocavitary fistulas are a recognized but uncommon complication of bacterial endocarditis. Infection extends from the valve leaflet into the surrounding myocardial tissue, eventually eroding through and creating an abnormal channel between the aortic root and an adjacent chamber, in this case the right ventricle. The direction and timing of flow through any intracardiac shunt is determined by the pressure gradient between the two communicating chambers. Aortic root pressure is roughly 120 mm Hg in systole and remains around 80 mm Hg in diastole. Right ventricular pressure is roughly 25 mm Hg in systole and only about 2 mm Hg in diastole. Because aortic pressure greatly exceeds RV pressure throughout both phases of the cycle, blood shunts continuously from aorta to RV, generating a continuous murmur on auscultation. The same logic explains continuous flow through any other AV fistula or PDA where pressure differential persists across both phases.

distractor_notes.B: Diastolic-only flow would be expected if aortic pressure exceeded RV pressure only in diastole, but the gradient persists during systole as well
distractor_notes.C: Systolic-only flow would mean RV systolic pressure briefly exceeded aortic systolic, which is not the case
distractor_notes.D, E, F: Pressure in the aortic root is higher than RV pressure throughout the cycle, so flow direction from RV to aorta is impossible

stem_paraphrase: A 52-year-old man comes to the office reporting two weeks of progressive shortness of breath and a dry cough. He has noticed reduced exercise tolerance that now limits his daily activities. A Doppler probe positioned at the cardiac apex captures the diastolic blood flow pattern across his mitral valve. The recording shows an early rapid filling peak (E wave) followed by a period of low flow (diastasis), then a second peak from atrial contraction (A wave), which ends as the mitral valve closes. An arrow on the tracing points to a position just after the E wave peak. The arrow location best corresponds to which of the following auscultation findings?
image_description: A graph of blood flow (across the mitral valve) versus time during diastole, with two characteristic peaks. The first peak (E wave) represents passive ventricular filling immediately after mitral valve opening. The second peak (A wave) represents the active filling contribution from atrial contraction. Between them is a low-flow phase (diastasis). The A wave terminates at the next S1 (mitral closure). An arrow points to the moment just after the E wave, during the deceleration of early rapid filling.
explanation_gist: The arrow marks the moment when early rapid passive filling of the LV abruptly decelerates. A third heart sound (S3) is generated by the sudden limitation of ventricular wall motion during this phase of passive filling. The sound is low-pitched and best heard with the bell of the stethoscope at the cardiac apex with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position. In healthy adults under 40 an S3 can be a normal finding. After age 40 an S3 implies abnormal LV cavity enlargement (eccentric hypertrophy), as occurs in chronic severe mitral regurgitation, chronic severe aortic regurgitation, or dilated cardiomyopathy. It may also be heard in high-output states (pregnancy, thyrotoxicosis). The patient's reduced exercise tolerance and dyspnea suggest cardiac dysfunction; combined with the late-life timing this S3 likely reflects a pathologic eccentric remodeling of the ventricle.

distractor_notes.A: S4 is a low-frequency late-diastolic sound coinciding with atrial systole (the A wave), reflecting blood striking a stiff non-compliant ventricle. It precedes S1 and is associated with concentric LV hypertrophy
distractor_notes.B: A mid-systolic click occurs in mitral valve prolapse, not during diastole
distractor_notes.C: S2 marks the closure of the semilunar valves at the end of systole, not during diastolic mitral inflow
distractor_notes.E: An aortic ejection click occurs in early systole at the start of ejection, not during diastole

scope_note: Pulmonary fluid analysis tangentially tied to CVS (heart failure cause). Allan to decide whether to include in final PDF.
stem_paraphrase: Following the cardiac evaluation that suggested heart failure, the same 52-year-old man is examined further. Jugular venous pressure is elevated, breath sounds are decreased at the right lung base with dullness to percussion, and there is 2+ bilateral lower extremity pitting edema. Chest x-ray shows cephalization of the pulmonary vessels, Kerley B lines, and a right-sided pleural effusion. Thoracentesis is performed and the fluid is analyzed. Compared with plasma values, which of the following findings is most likely in this patient's pleural fluid?
options_paraphrase: B. High protein content (greater than serum)
C. High white blood cell count
explanation_gist: Pleural effusions are classified as transudates or exudates using the Light criteria, which compare pleural protein and LDH to serum levels. A transudate forms when an imbalance in hydrostatic or oncotic forces drives fluid across an otherwise intact capillary membrane. In heart failure, elevated pulmonary venous and capillary hydrostatic pressure pushes a protein-poor and LDH-poor ultrafiltrate into the pleural space, so the fluid has low protein and low LDH relative to serum. An exudate forms when increased capillary permeability (infection, malignancy, inflammation) allows protein and large molecules to leak into the space, producing high protein and high LDH levels. Heart failure is the most common cause of transudative effusion; pneumonia, malignancy, tuberculosis, and rheumatologic disease are common causes of exudate. The Light criteria flag an effusion as exudative if pleural-to-serum protein ratio exceeds 0.5, pleural-to-serum LDH ratio exceeds 0.6, or pleural LDH is more than two-thirds of the upper limit of normal serum LDH.

distractor_notes.A: High amylase suggests pancreatitis, pancreatic pseudocyst, or esophageal rupture
distractor_notes.B: High pleural protein indicates an exudative process such as infection or malignancy, not a transudate from heart failure
distractor_notes.C: High WBC content suggests a parapneumonic or malignant effusion, not transudate
distractor_notes.D: Low pleural glucose is characteristic of bacterial infection or malignancy, where cells consume glucose

stem_paraphrase: A 12-day-old girl is evaluated in the neonatal intensive care unit for a heart murmur. She was born at 25 weeks gestation and was admitted to the NICU for respiratory support. Her 21-year-old mother had preeclampsia during pregnancy. Cardiac examination reveals a continuous murmur at the left sternal border and bounding femoral and palmar pulses. Echocardiography confirms the diagnosis. Compared with a normal newborn, which of the following combinations of changes in left ventricular output, systemic vascular resistance, and left atrial filling is most likely present?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased LV output, decreased SVR, decreased LA filling
B. Decreased LV output, decreased SVR, increased LA filling
C. Increased LV output, decreased SVR, increased LA filling
D. Increased LV output, increased SVR, increased LA filling
E. Increased LV output, increased SVR, decreased LA filling
explanation_gist: The continuous murmur, bounding peripheral pulses, and history of extreme prematurity point to a patent ductus arteriosus. Postnatally the ductus normally closes within hours to days as oxygenation rises and circulating prostaglandin levels fall; preterm infants frequently fail this closure. A persistent PDA provides a low-resistance shortcut from the aorta back into the pulmonary artery. Three integrated changes follow. First, the parallel low-resistance pulmonary pathway reduces overall systemic vascular resistance (the LV faces less downstream resistance). Second, the shunted blood traverses the pulmonary vasculature and returns through the pulmonary veins to the left atrium, increasing left atrial filling and LV preload. Third, the combination of reduced afterload and increased preload raises stroke volume and therefore LV output. The wide pulse pressure (high stroke volume + low diastolic from continuous aorta-to-PA runoff) accounts for the bounding pulses. If the shunt remains large and chronic, the volume-overloaded LV can eventually fail, at which point CO drops and SVR rises reflexively, and bounding pulses disappear.

distractor_notes.A, B, D, E: PDA initially causes the triad of increased LV output, decreased SVR, and increased LA filling. Only option C captures all three. Reduced CO or increased SVR would represent later-stage decompensation or compensatory response after LV failure, neither of which is present in this acutely diagnosed infant

stem_paraphrase: A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation of a heart murmur. He has no chest pain, palpitations, dizziness, or shortness of breath at rest. He exercises regularly without any abnormal symptoms. Cardiac auscultation in the second right intercostal space reveals an ejection click followed by a soft systolic murmur that radiates to the neck. A simultaneous tracing of left ventricular pressure and volume across one cardiac cycle is shown, with five labeled points (A, B, C, D, E) corresponding to specific moments in the cycle. Which of the labeled points corresponds to the opening of the valve most likely affected in this patient?
image_description: A two-curve plot of LV pressure (upper trace, mm Hg) and LV volume (lower trace, mL) versus time across one cardiac cycle. Five labeled points: A at end of atrial systole (slight rise in volume), B where LV pressure rises sharply and rapidly crosses the aortic diastolic pressure (start of ejection), C at peak LV pressure (end of ejection / aortic closure), D at the early diastolic point of mitral valve opening, E during passive filling.
explanation_gist: The clinical picture (asymptomatic young man, ejection click at the right second intercostal space, soft systolic murmur radiating to the neck) is most consistent with a bicuspid aortic valve, a common congenital abnormality often asymptomatic in young adulthood. The question asks where on the LV pressure tracing the aortic valve opens. Aortic opening occurs at the end of isovolumetric contraction, when LV pressure has risen high enough to exceed the central aortic pressure and forward ejection begins. On the LV pressure curve, this corresponds to the moment when LV pressure stops climbing in isolation and starts driving forward flow into the aorta, which is the inflection at the upper end of the isovolumetric contraction segment. This is point B. The opposite event (aortic closure at the end of ejection) marks point C, and the start and end of mitral filling correspond to D and E respectively.

distractor_notes.A: This point marks atrial systole and the small ventricular volume rise it produces, immediately before mitral valve closure begins isovolumetric contraction
distractor_notes.C: Aortic valve closure, where LV pressure has dropped below aortic pressure and isovolumetric relaxation begins
distractor_notes.D: Mitral valve opens at the end of isovolumetric relaxation, marking the start of LV filling
distractor_notes.E: Passive filling phase of diastole, no valve event

stem_paraphrase: A 43-year-old man is evaluated for episodes of palpitations that occur during periods of anxiety. He describes a sudden onset of pounding in the chest associated with lightheadedness and shortness of breath. He has never lost consciousness. He has no known heart disease and an unremarkable family history. After initial evaluation, he is started on verapamil and reports a clear reduction in the frequency of his palpitation episodes. A tracing of the electrical activity of a specific type of cardiac cell from this patient is shown. Which of the following effects would verapamil most likely have on these cells?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased excitation-contraction coupling within the cell
B. Increased peak amplitude of the action potential
C. Lowered threshold potential for action potential initiation
D. Reduced refractory period after each action potential
E. Slowed spontaneous depolarization during phase 4
image_description: A pacemaker action potential tracing from a slow-response cardiac cell (SA or AV nodal type). Resting/maximum diastolic potential around -60 mV with a gentle upward slope during phase 4 (spontaneous depolarization), a slow phase 0 upstroke (because the upstroke depends on L-type Ca influx rather than fast Na current), absence of a plateau, and phase 3 repolarization back to maximum diastolic potential.
explanation_gist: The patient's anxiety-provoked sudden palpitations with lightheadedness fit paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia. PSVT typically arises from a reentrant circuit involving the AV node. The tracing shown is a pacemaker action potential of a slow-response tissue (SA or AV node), where the upstroke and resting potential both depend on slow inward L-type calcium current. Verapamil is a Class IV antiarrhythmic and an L-type calcium channel blocker. In slow-response tissue it produces two key effects: it slows the rate of phase 4 spontaneous depolarization (reducing the slope so threshold is reached later) and it slows the phase 0 upstroke. Together these reduce AV nodal conduction velocity and increase nodal refractoriness, breaking or preventing AV nodal reentrant circuits. This makes verapamil effective at terminating and preventing recurrent PSVT. The same calcium blockade in working ventricular myocytes would reduce inotropy by limiting calcium availability for contraction, but the question stem shows a nodal action potential, not a ventricular one.

distractor_notes.A: Reducing excitation-contraction coupling does occur with verapamil in ventricular myocytes (reduced inotropy), but the tracing shown is a pacemaker AP from nodal tissue, where coupling and contractility are not relevant
distractor_notes.B: Class I and IV agents do not raise AP amplitude; they generally reduce upstroke amplitude in their target tissue
distractor_notes.C: Verapamil does not lower threshold; if anything it raises the threshold/decreases excitability in nodal cells
distractor_notes.D: Verapamil increases nodal refractory period rather than reducing it; this is part of its therapeutic effect

stem_paraphrase: A 39-year-old man comes to the office reporting two weeks of progressive fatigue. He has no prior medical conditions but had a self-limited upper respiratory illness recently. He does not use tobacco, alcohol, or recreational drugs and has not traveled recently. Temperature is 37.2 C, blood pressure is 116/66 mm Hg, pulse is 100 per minute, and respirations are 20 per minute. Physical examination reveals bibasilar lung crackles. Echocardiography demonstrates no valvular abnormalities and provides the following measurements: left ventricular end-diastolic volume 125 mL, left ventricular end-systolic volume 75 mL. Based on these findings, which of the following statements about this patient is most accurate?
options_paraphrase: C. Left ventricular ejection fraction is 40%
D. Left ventricular ejection fraction is 60%
explanation_gist: Three basic cardiac output relationships solve this question. Stroke volume equals end-diastolic volume minus end-systolic volume: 125 − 75 = 50 mL. Ejection fraction equals stroke volume divided by end-diastolic volume, multiplied by 100 to get a percentage: (50 / 125) × 100 = 40%. Normal LV ejection fraction is approximately 50% or greater, so 40% indicates reduced systolic function (heart failure with reduced ejection fraction). Cardiac output equals stroke volume multiplied by heart rate: 50 mL × 100 beats/min = 5 L/min, which is within the normal range. The patient has clinically masked systolic dysfunction: low stroke volume is being temporarily compensated by an elevated heart rate to maintain cardiac output, but symptoms (fatigue, crackles) are emerging. The recent viral upper respiratory illness raises suspicion for postviral nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy or myocarditis.

distractor_notes.A: Cardiac output is 5 L/min (50 × 100), not 4
distractor_notes.B: Cardiac output is 5 L/min, not 6
distractor_notes.D: 60% would represent a preserved EF; this patient is at 40%
distractor_notes.E: Stroke volume is 50 mL (125 - 75), not 40

stem_paraphrase: A 67-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his son after a syncopal episode. The son was helping his father clean out the garage when the father said he felt dizzy. As the son was helping him into a chair, the patient lost consciousness. He awoke spontaneously about a minute later without disorientation or confusion. ECG demonstrates bradycardia with a regular rhythm and narrow QRS complexes. There is complete dissociation between the P waves and QRS complexes. Which of the following locations is most likely pacing this patient's ventricles?
explanation_gist: Complete dissociation of P waves from QRS complexes defines third-degree (complete) AV block. The SA node continues to fire at its intrinsic rate (around 60 to 100 per minute), generating P waves, but no impulses reach the ventricles because the block is somewhere between the atria and the ventricular conducting system. The ventricles are then paced by whichever latent pacemaker lies just below the block. Each level of the conduction system has a progressively slower intrinsic rate and produces a different QRS morphology. A junctional/His bundle escape rate is around 40 to 60 per minute and produces a narrow QRS because the impulse still travels through the normal bundle branches and Purkinje system to depolarize the ventricles synchronously. A more distal escape from the bundle branches or Purkinje system fires at 25 to 40 per minute and produces a wide QRS because at least one ventricle is depolarized cell-to-cell rather than through the rapid conduction system. The narrow-QRS escape in this patient places the block at the AV node or in the upper His bundle, with His bundle cells driving the ventricles.

distractor_notes.B: A pacemaker in the left bundle branch would produce a wide QRS because impulse spread would not use both ventricles' rapid conduction system simultaneously
distractor_notes.C: Ventricular muscle itself does not normally exhibit pacemaker automaticity at meaningful rates
distractor_notes.D: Purkinje system escape produces a wide QRS at a slower rate (25 to 40 per minute), not the narrow QRS shown
distractor_notes.E: SA node pacing would give a normal sinus rhythm with each P wave followed by a QRS, not the dissociation seen here

stem_paraphrase: A 44-year-old man comes to the office with progressive shortness of breath. He must now stop halfway up the stairs to his second-floor apartment to catch his breath. He has a history of nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy and chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. A new medication is initiated that improves his symptoms by increasing urine output and reducing peripheral vascular resistance. The drug works by inhibiting a metalloprotease, thereby prolonging the action of certain endogenous polypeptides. These polypeptides are most likely secreted by which of the following cell types?
explanation_gist: The medication described is a neprilysin inhibitor (sacubitril, typically combined with the angiotensin receptor blocker valsartan as ARNI therapy). Neprilysin is a membrane-bound metalloprotease that degrades several endogenous vasoactive peptides, the most clinically important of which are the natriuretic peptides. Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) is secreted by atrial cardiomyocytes in response to atrial stretch, and B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) is secreted by ventricular cardiomyocytes in response to myocardial wall stress. Both are released as the failing heart is volume- and pressure-loaded. Once released, ANP and BNP bind natriuretic peptide receptors on target tissues, activate guanylate cyclase, raise intracellular cGMP, and produce: renal afferent arteriolar dilation with efferent constriction (increased GFR), inhibition of sodium reabsorption in the collecting duct, inhibition of renin and aldosterone secretion (counteracting RAAS), and systemic arteriolar vasodilation. Inhibiting neprilysin prolongs the half-life of endogenously secreted ANP and BNP, amplifying these beneficial effects in heart failure.

distractor_notes.A: Zona glomerulosa cells secrete aldosterone; their secretion is suppressed (not enhanced) by neprilysin inhibition, indirectly via the ANP/BNP effect on RAAS
distractor_notes.C: Hepatic stellate cells regulate local sinusoidal blood flow but have no significant role in systemic neurohormonal regulation of heart failure
distractor_notes.D: Purkinje fibers conduct electrical impulses and do not have endocrine function
distractor_notes.E: Juxtaglomerular cells secrete renin; this is the RAAS axis that ARNI inhibits indirectly, not the natriuretic peptide axis it enhances
distractor_notes.F: Endothelial cells release vasoactive mediators like NO and endothelin but are not the target of neprilysin therapy

stem_paraphrase: A 14-year-old girl is brought to the office to establish medical care. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiac examination reveals a murmur best heard at the left upper sternal border. A phonocardiogram obtained at this location shows the following findings: S1, an early-peaking systolic ejection murmur (SM), and a second heart sound (S2) that splits widely into aortic (A2) and pulmonic (P2) components, with the split duration not changing between expiration and inspiration. This patient's murmur is most likely due to which of the following mechanisms?
options_paraphrase: A. Abnormal narrowing of the aortic valve (aortic stenosis)
B. Incomplete development of the ventricular septum (VSD)
C. Increased blood flow to the lungs across a normal pulmonic valve
D. Regurgitation of blood from the aorta into the left ventricle (aortic insufficiency)
image_description: Two side-by-side phonocardiogram strips covering an expiration phase and an inspiration phase. Each cardiac cycle shows S1, an early-peaking diamond-shaped systolic ejection murmur (SM), and a clearly split S2 with separate A2 and P2 components. The interval between A2 and P2 does not change between expiration and inspiration (widely split, fixed S2).
explanation_gist: A widely split, fixed S2 combined with a soft early-systolic ejection murmur over the left upper sternal border is the classic auscultatory signature of an atrial septal defect. Left atrial pressure is normally slightly higher than right atrial pressure because the thin-walled right atrium is more compliant; an ASD therefore creates a left-to-right shunt at the atrial level. This shunt delivers extra blood volume to the right atrium and right ventricle on every cardiac cycle. The murmur itself is not produced by flow across the ASD (low velocity, no turbulence, silent) but by the increased volume of blood traversing the normal pulmonic valve, generating a pulmonic flow ejection murmur. The wide and fixed splitting of S2 reflects two changes: the increased RV volume delays pulmonic valve closure (widening the A2-P2 gap), and because the chronically elevated RV venous return is supplied partly by the shunt rather than by inspiration-dependent venous return, the normal inspiratory widening of the split disappears (it is now fixed across the respiratory cycle). A larger ASD can also generate a soft diastolic rumble at the lower left sternal border from increased tricuspid flow, not seen here.

distractor_notes.A: Aortic stenosis would produce a crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border with possible paradoxical splitting of S2, not wide fixed splitting at the left upper sternal border
distractor_notes.B: A VSD typically produces a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border due to a high-pressure shunt; splitting of S2 may widen but is not usually fixed because RV volume does not increase as constantly as with an ASD
distractor_notes.D: Aortic insufficiency causes a decrescendo diastolic murmur, not a systolic murmur, and does not produce fixed splitting of S2

stem_paraphrase: A 38-year-old woman is evaluated for shortness of breath. Over the past several months she has had poor exercise tolerance that she initially attributed to deconditioning. Over the past several weeks her symptoms have worsened, and she is now short of breath even with mild exertion. There is no family history of heart disease. Right and left heart catheterization yields the following values: left ventricular end-diastolic pressure 10 mm Hg (normal 3 to 12), left ventricular peak systolic pressure 110 mm Hg (normal 100 to 140), and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 36 mm Hg (normal less than 12). Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
explanation_gist: Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure approximates left atrial pressure: the catheter occludes a distal pulmonary artery branch, creating a static column of blood between the tip and the left atrium so that the measured pressure tracks LA pressure. In healthy hearts, LA pressure equals LV end-diastolic pressure during diastole, because the open mitral valve presents minimal resistance to flow between the two chambers. This patient has a normal LVEDP (10) but a markedly elevated PCWP (36), creating a large diastolic pressure gradient between the left atrium (high) and the LV (normal). The only way to develop this gradient is mechanical obstruction at the mitral valve: stenosis. In mitral stenosis (most commonly from prior rheumatic fever), thickened and partially fused leaflets restrict flow during diastole. Blood backs up into the LA, raising LA pressure (and PCWP) while the LV is partially shielded from the elevated upstream pressures. Symptoms reflect both reduced forward flow (exertional dyspnea, fatigue) and pulmonary congestion from elevated pulmonary venous and capillary pressures.

distractor_notes.A: Aortic stenosis would raise LV peak systolic pressure substantially above the normal aortic systolic pressure, often well over 200, which is not seen here
distractor_notes.B: Tamponade equalizes diastolic pressures across all four chambers; LVEDP and PCWP would both be elevated to similar values, not the discrepant pattern shown
distractor_notes.C: Dilated cardiomyopathy raises LVEDP as the failing LV cannot accept normal venous return; PCWP rises in parallel, producing no LA-LV gradient
distractor_notes.E: Restrictive cardiomyopathy also elevates LVEDP from impaired diastolic filling, again producing a parallel rise in PCWP without the large gradient seen here

stem_paraphrase: A 23-year-old man comes to the emergency department with sudden onset palpitations that began while he was at his desk at work. He has no known medical problems and does not use tobacco or recreational drugs. He drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends. Initial blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg and pulse is 160 per minute and regular. Gentle massage applied just below the angle of the right mandible produces immediate improvement of his condition. His blood pressure is now 120/80 mm Hg and pulse is 75 per minute. Which of the following mechanisms is responsible for the improvement?
options_paraphrase: B. Increased sympathetic output to the sinoatrial node
D. Prolonged refractory period of the atrioventricular node
E. Prolonged refractory period of the ventricular myocardium
explanation_gist: The clinical picture (sudden onset of a rapid regular narrow-complex tachycardia in a young patient, terminated by carotid sinus massage) is consistent with paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, most commonly AV nodal reentrant tachycardia. The reentrant circuit cycles through the slow and fast pathways within or near the AV node. Carotid sinus massage compresses the carotid sinus, which contains stretch-sensitive baroreceptors. This mechanically increases baroreceptor afferent firing, which is interpreted by central brainstem cardiovascular centers as elevated blood pressure. The reflex response is increased parasympathetic outflow via the vagus nerve to the heart and decreased sympathetic outflow. Parasympathetic effects on the AV node include slowing of conduction velocity and lengthening of the refractory period. The longer refractory period prevents the reentrant impulse from completing its circuit, breaking the tachycardia and restoring sinus rhythm. Other vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, cold water immersion, gagging) work by the same mechanism.

distractor_notes.A: Carotid sinus massage INCREASES baroreceptor firing, not decreases it
distractor_notes.B: Sympathetic output is reduced (not increased) by the vagal reflex triggered here; increased sympathetic activity would speed AV node conduction and prolong the arrhythmia
distractor_notes.C: SVR drops modestly with the parasympathetic reflex; the relevant target is the AV node, not the vasculature
distractor_notes.E: Vagal innervation of the ventricular myocardium is sparse and does not significantly affect ventricular refractoriness

stem_paraphrase: A 56-year-old man comes to the emergency department for palpitations that began several hours ago. He has not had similar symptoms before. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, lightheadedness, or syncope. He drinks alcohol on weekends and does not use recreational drugs. There is no family history of heart disease. BP is 145/78 mm Hg and BMI is 40 kg/m2. ECG is shown (note: the strip demonstrates rapid regular atrial activity in a saw-toothed pattern with narrow QRS complexes and a regular ventricular response). He is medically managed in the ED and is then referred to the electrophysiology laboratory for radiofrequency catheter ablation to terminate his arrhythmia. The ablation procedure should create a conduction block through which of the following areas?
options_paraphrase: A. An accessory pathway that bypasses the atrioventricular node
B. The area of right atrium between the tricuspid valve annulus and the inferior vena cava (cavotricuspid isthmus)
C. The right ventricular outflow tract
D. The ostia of the pulmonary veins in the left atrium
E. The slow pathway of the atrioventricular node
explanation_gist: The ECG description (rapid, regular atrial activity in a saw-toothed flutter wave pattern with narrow QRS complexes and a regular ventricular response) is diagnostic of typical atrial flutter. The atrial rate is around 300 per minute; ventricular rate depends on AV nodal conduction ratio (often 2:1 giving 150, or 3:1 giving 100, or 4:1 giving 75). Typical atrial flutter is sustained by a single large reentrant circuit that travels counterclockwise around the right atrium, passing through the cavotricuspid isthmus (the narrow band of atrial tissue bounded by the tricuspid valve annulus and the inferior vena cava). This isthmus is the obligate slow-conduction segment of the circuit; interrupting it with a linear lesion blocks the reentry and abolishes the arrhythmia. Radiofrequency ablation across the cavotricuspid isthmus is highly effective for typical atrial flutter, with success rates exceeding 90%. The procedure is mapped under fluoroscopy or with electroanatomic guidance.

distractor_notes.A: Accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) ablation targets atrioventricular reciprocating tachycardia in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, characterized by a short PR and delta wave, not seen in atrial flutter
distractor_notes.C: The RV outflow tract is the site of origin for idiopathic ventricular tachycardia, presenting with wide QRS and uniform morphology
distractor_notes.D: Pulmonary vein ostia are the ectopic trigger sites for atrial fibrillation; pulmonary vein isolation is the ablation strategy for symptomatic AF, which has irregularly irregular R-R intervals and no organized atrial activity
distractor_notes.E: The slow pathway of the AV node is the target for AVNRT ablation, which presents as narrow regular tachycardia with retrograde P waves, not the saw-tooth flutter pattern

stem_paraphrase: A 69-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with sudden onset palpitations and dyspnea. His medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease. ECG reveals a heart rate of 120 per minute with an irregularly irregular rhythm, narrow QRS complexes, and no organized P waves. Which of the following physiologic factors most likely determines the ventricular contraction rate in this patient?
explanation_gist: The ECG findings define atrial fibrillation: irregularly irregular R-R intervals, absent organized P waves, and rapid disorganized fibrillatory atrial activity that proceeds at 400 to 600 per minute. The atria fire chaotically, and the ventricles are protected from this barrage by the filtering function of the AV node. Each impulse that reaches the AV node leaves it in a refractory state for a finite period, during which subsequent atrial impulses cannot be transmitted. The number of impulses that successfully cross to the ventricles per unit time, and therefore the ventricular contraction rate, is determined by how long the AV node remains refractory between successful conductions. Untreated AF typically conducts to give a ventricular rate of 90 to 170 per minute. Pharmacologic therapy that prolongs AV node refractoriness (beta-blockers, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, digoxin) slows the ventricular rate. Atrial rate, SA node activity, and bundle/Purkinje properties do not gate ventricular response in AF.

distractor_notes.A: Atrial depolarization is occurring at 400 to 600 per minute, but the ventricles cannot follow this rate because the AV node filters the impulses
distractor_notes.C: Bundle branch conductivity affects QRS width and morphology (wide QRS in bundle branch block), not the ventricular rate
distractor_notes.D: Purkinje pacemaker activity emerges only when the SA and AV nodes are completely silent (severe bradycardia); it does not determine the rate in fast-conducting AF
distractor_notes.E: The SA node is bypassed in AF; the atria fibrillate from ectopic foci independent of SA node firing
distractor_notes.F: Ventricular refractoriness allows much faster rates than seen here (up to 300 in VT); it is not the rate-limiting factor in AF

stem_paraphrase: A 24-year-old healthy woman participates in a pharmacologic clinical study. Various cardiovascular parameters are measured while she is resting in an upright position. Baseline values are plotted on a combined cardiac function curve (Frank-Starling curve) and vascular function curve (venous return curve), with cardiac output on the y-axis and right atrial pressure on the x-axis. She then receives a moderate dose of intravenous nitroglycerin by continuous infusion, and the measurements are repeated. Which of the following changes in the cardiac and vascular function curves is most likely to be observed?
options_paraphrase: A. Both curves shifted leftward (increased contractility, increased venous return)
B. Cardiac function curve shifted rightward (decreased contractility), vascular function curve shifted leftward
C. Both curves shifted rightward (decreased contractility, decreased venous return)
D. Cardiac function curve unchanged, vascular function curve shifted leftward (decreased venous return at any given RA pressure)
E. Cardiac function curve shifted leftward (increased contractility), vascular function curve shifted rightward (increased venous return)
image_description: A baseline plot shows the cardiac function (Frank-Starling) curve in red rising upward as right atrial pressure increases, intersecting with the vascular function (venous return) curve in blue that descends as right atrial pressure rises. The intersection point on the plot represents the steady-state cardiac output and right atrial pressure. Each answer option overlays a dashed pair of curves representing post-intervention states, demonstrating different combinations of leftward/rightward shifts.
explanation_gist: These coupled curves describe the steady-state interaction between the heart and the vasculature. The cardiac function curve maps cardiac output against right atrial pressure (preload) at constant contractility; shifts left or right reflect changes in inotropy. The vascular function curve maps venous return against right atrial pressure at constant blood volume and venous tone; shifts left or right reflect changes in either total blood volume or unstressed/stressed venous capacitance. Nitroglycerin is primarily a venodilator: it relaxes venous smooth muscle, expanding venous capacitance. As a result, more blood pools in the now-larger venous reservoir, less blood reaches the right atrium at any given right atrial pressure, and the mean systemic filling pressure (the x-axis intercept of the venous return curve, the pressure at which net venous return is zero) drops. The vascular function curve shifts leftward. The cardiac function curve is unchanged because nitroglycerin has no direct inotropic effect.
