A new intersection point develops at a lower right atrial pressure and lower cardiac output. This effect underlies the use of nitroglycerin to relieve symptoms in conditions of elevated preload (acute pulmonary edema, anginal preload reduction).

distractor_notes.A: Leftward shift of the cardiac function curve indicates increased contractility (e.g. digoxin), and rightward shift of vascular function curve indicates increased venous return (e.g. fluid bolus). Nitroglycerin does neither
distractor_notes.B: Rightward shift of cardiac function curve fits a negative inotrope (e.g. verapamil); nitroglycerin does not depress contractility
distractor_notes.C: Decreased contractility plus decreased venous return is not the nitroglycerin pattern; pure venodilation leaves contractility unchanged
distractor_notes.E: Increased contractility plus increased venous return describes exercise, not nitroglycerin

stem_paraphrase: A 50-year-old man comes to the emergency department for intermittent palpitations over the last 6 months. He describes the episodes as very uncomfortable and says they occur randomly, last for several hours, and resolve spontaneously or with sleep. The current episode began an hour ago and has been accompanied by lightheadedness. He has no medical history. Temperature is normal, blood pressure is 122/75 mm Hg, and BMI is 30. Chest examination reveals no murmurs and clear lung fields. There is trace peripheral edema. A cardiac rhythm strip obtained in the ED shows an irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves. Aberrant electrical activity in which of the following anatomic structures is the most likely trigger for this patient's condition?
explanation_gist: The intermittent self-resolving episodes of irregularly irregular palpitations with no organized P waves on the rhythm strip define paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. The development of AF requires both a substrate (electrical remodeling of the atrial myocardium, with shortened refractory periods and heterogeneous conduction) and a trigger (a discrete focus of aberrant electrical activity that initiates the chaotic atrial rhythm). The most common anatomic source of these triggering foci is the muscular sleeves of myocytes that extend from the left atrium into the proximal pulmonary veins. These sites are prone to spontaneous ectopic firing, particularly under conditions of increased atrial stretch, sympathetic surge, or after alcohol exposure. Each ectopic discharge can initiate a new round of AF in the remodeled atrium. Catheter ablation that electrically isolates the pulmonary vein ostia from the rest of the left atrium (pulmonary vein isolation) is the cornerstone of invasive therapy for symptomatic paroxysmal AF, as it interrupts the trigger-to-substrate pathway.

distractor_notes.A: AV nodal reentry causes AVNRT with regular narrow QRS tachycardia, not irregularly irregular AF
distractor_notes.B: The crista terminalis can be involved in atrial flutter reentry, which shows saw-tooth flutter waves and a regular ventricular response, not irregular AF
distractor_notes.C: Papillary muscles can give rise to idiopathic ventricular tachycardia (wide QRS, monomorphic), not AF
distractor_notes.E: RV outflow tract is another idiopathic VT origin, not AF
distractor_notes.F: The cavotricuspid isthmus near the tricuspid annulus is the atrial flutter reentry site, not AF

stem_paraphrase: A 20-year-old previously healthy woman is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision. She was a restrained rear-seat passenger when another car struck the side door. There was extensive damage to the vehicle, requiring prolonged extrication. On arrival, blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg and pulse is 120 per minute. On physical examination she is alert but appears anxious, pale, and diaphoretic. There is no obvious head or neck injury. Lungs are clear bilaterally and auscultation reveals tachycardia. The abdomen is soft. Several actively bleeding lacerations are present on the lower extremities. X-rays show pelvic and left femoral shaft fractures. Which of the following combinations of cardiac contractility, baroreceptor firing rate, and atrial natriuretic peptide release is most likely present in this patient?
options_paraphrase: A. Increased contractility, increased baroreceptor firing, increased ANP release
B. Decreased contractility, increased baroreceptor firing, increased ANP release
C. Increased contractility, decreased baroreceptor firing, increased ANP release
D. Decreased contractility, increased baroreceptor firing, decreased ANP release
E. Increased contractility, decreased baroreceptor firing, decreased ANP release
F. Decreased contractility, decreased baroreceptor firing, decreased ANP release
G. Decreased contractility, decreased baroreceptor firing, increased ANP release
H. Increased contractility, increased baroreceptor firing, decreased ANP release
explanation_gist: Hypotension and tachycardia in the setting of multiple lacerations and pelvic fracture (a common source of severe internal bleeding) indicate hypovolemic shock from acute hemorrhage. Three integrated changes follow. First, the loss of intravascular volume reduces arterial blood pressure, which mechanically reduces stretch on the carotid and aortic baroreceptors and lowers their afferent firing rate. The brainstem cardiovascular control center interprets reduced baroreceptor input as low blood pressure and removes the tonic inhibition it normally places on sympathetic outflow; sympathetic activity to the heart and vasculature rises while parasympathetic outflow falls. Second, increased sympathetic input to the ventricular myocardium increases cardiac contractility via beta-1 receptor activation (the same input also raises heart rate at the SA node and constricts peripheral vessels to maintain BP). Third, atrial natriuretic peptide is normally released by atrial cardiomyocytes in response to atrial wall stretch. With acute volume loss, atrial stretch falls and ANP release drops. The integrated profile in hemorrhagic shock is therefore: increased contractility, decreased baroreceptor firing, and decreased ANP release.

distractor_notes.A, F, G, H: Baroreceptor firing is inversely related to cardiac contractility because reduced baroreceptor input releases sympathetic outflow that drives inotropy; combinations where both move in the same direction are physiologically inconsistent
distractor_notes.B: Loss of volume reduces atrial stretch and therefore ANP secretion; increased ANP would only occur with hypervolemia
distractor_notes.C, D: Same atrial stretch reasoning rules out increased ANP in this setting

stem_paraphrase: A 62-year-old woman comes to the emergency department for palpitations and shortness of breath that began an hour ago. Her medical history includes hypertension, coronary artery disease, and paroxysmal atrial flutter. Blood pressure is 108/76 mm Hg and pulse is rapid. ECG shows atrial flutter with a ventricular rate of approximately 150 per minute. The estimated atrial rate based on the ECG flutter waves is 300 per minute. Which of the following is most likely responsible for the rate difference between the atria and the ventricles?
options_paraphrase: A. Calcium current within the atrioventricular node
B. Calcium current within the sinoatrial node
C. Sodium current within an atrioventricular accessory pathway
D. Sodium current within the atria
E. Sodium current within the ventricles
image_description: Two action potential tracings shown for comparison. The top tracing is a slow-response (pacemaker) action potential typical of the SA and AV nodes: relatively depolarized resting potential, gentle phase 4 slope from slow inward calcium, slow phase 0 driven by L-type calcium current, no plateau. The bottom tracing is a fast-response (nonpacemaker) ventricular myocyte action potential: more negative resting potential, abrupt phase 0 from fast sodium influx, prominent plateau from L-type calcium, repolarization driven by potassium efflux.
explanation_gist: Atrial flutter generates atrial impulses at approximately 300 per minute through a large reentrant circuit, most commonly traversing the cavotricuspid isthmus. If every atrial impulse were transmitted directly to the ventricles, the ventricular rate would also reach 300, leaving inadequate diastolic filling time and producing hemodynamic collapse. The AV node prevents this by acting as a rate-limiting filter. AV nodal myocytes are slow-response tissue, meaning their phase 0 upstroke is driven by the slow inward L-type calcium current rather than the fast inward sodium current that powers atrial and ventricular myocyte depolarization. The slow calcium-dependent upstroke means each AV nodal cell requires more time to depolarize and to recover. The result is decrementally slower conduction and a long refractory period, so only a fraction of the rapid atrial impulses (commonly 1 in 2, giving the typical 2:1 flutter with ventricular rate 150) reach the ventricles. The same mechanism limits ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation, where atrial rates can reach 500 to 600 per minute.

distractor_notes.B: The SA node also uses slow calcium current for its action potential, but it is suppressed during atrial flutter; the relevant filter is downstream at the AV node
distractor_notes.C: An accessory pathway (bundle of Kent in Wolff-Parkinson-White) bypasses the AV node and uses fast sodium channels, so it does NOT slow rapid atrial rates; if present it can paradoxically allow dangerous rates to reach the ventricles
distractor_notes.D, E: Fast sodium channels in the atria and ventricles enable rapid impulse conduction, the opposite of the slow buffering effect needed; they cannot account for the AV slowing

stem_paraphrase: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to chronic fatigue, shortness of breath, and failure to gain weight. The child was recently adopted from an orphanage in Asia. He weighs 20 kg (44.2 lb). Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals a loud systolic murmur. Echocardiogram demonstrates pulmonic stenosis and a subaortic ventricular septal defect with rightward deviation of the origin of the aorta. The mother describes occasional episodes of severe dyspnea and cyanosis, during which the child quickly assumes a squatting position. Which of the following mechanisms during squatting relieves this child's symptoms?
explanation_gist: The four anatomic features (VSD, overriding aorta, pulmonic stenosis, RV hypertrophy) define Tetralogy of Fallot. The pulmonic stenosis creates fixed resistance to forward flow into the pulmonary artery, so the RV preferentially diverts deoxygenated blood across the VSD into the overriding aorta and out to the systemic circulation. The result is hypoxemia, especially during exertion or stress (Tet spells), when the right-to-left shunt worsens. The magnitude of right-to-left shunting depends on the ratio of pulmonary vascular resistance to systemic vascular resistance. If SVR rises relative to PVR, blood is forced from the high-pressure systemic side back into the lower-pressure pulmonary circulation, increasing pulmonary flow and improving oxygenation. Squatting compresses the femoral arteries and abdominal aorta, mechanically raising SVR within seconds. This restored SVR/PVR ratio drives more RV output into the lungs, raises arterial oxygen content, and aborts the Tet spell. Caregivers of infants who cannot squat can mimic the effect by holding the child in a knee-to-chest position.

distractor_notes.A: LV workload is not significantly altered by squatting; the relevant change is at the systemic vasculature, not the LV
distractor_notes.B: Pulmonary blood flow INCREASES with squatting, not decreases; this is part of why it works
distractor_notes.C: PVR is largely fixed in this patient by the structural pulmonic stenosis; squatting does not change PVR
distractor_notes.D: Lung compliance is not significantly altered by squatting; if anything, compressing the chest in the position slightly reduces lung volumes

stem_paraphrase: A 32-year-old woman comes to the outpatient clinic with progressive shortness of breath for the past 6 months. She can now only walk 3 to 4 blocks on level ground before becoming short of breath. She also reports an occasional dry cough at night. She denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, weight loss, or lower extremity swelling. She immigrated from Guatemala 8 years ago. Medical history is unremarkable. On examination she appears comfortable. Blood pressure is 128/75 mm Hg and pulse is 78 per minute. Peripheral pulses are full and symmetric. The lungs are clear. S1 is loud. With the patient in the left lateral decubitus position and breath held at the end of expiration, a mid- to end-diastolic murmur is heard. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable. Which of the following are the most likely left ventricular hemodynamic findings at rest in this patient (LV diastolic pressure, afterload, and contractility)?
options_paraphrase: A. Increased LV diastolic pressure, increased afterload, decreased contractility
B. Increased LV diastolic pressure, increased afterload, normal contractility
C. Normal LV diastolic pressure, normal afterload, decreased contractility
D. Normal LV diastolic pressure, normal afterload, normal contractility
explanation_gist: The combination of immigration from Latin America, slowly progressive exertional dyspnea, a loud S1, and a mid- to late-diastolic apical murmur best heard in the left lateral decubitus position is classic for mitral stenosis from chronic rheumatic heart disease. The key insight for this question is that the LV itself is upstream of the mitral obstruction in a hemodynamic sense: the obstruction is at the inflow to the LV, not in the LV or beyond it. In mild-to-moderate MS at rest, the LV receives a restricted but still tolerable volume of blood; LV end-diastolic pressure is normal (and may even be slightly low because of impaired filling), afterload is normal (no systemic resistance change), and contractility is normal (the muscle itself is intact). What is abnormal is upstream: left atrial pressure is elevated, generating the diastolic pressure gradient that drives blood across the stenotic valve. With exertion the higher cardiac output demand exposes the limitation, producing dyspnea. As MS becomes severe over years, LV filling can become persistently inadequate, sometimes reducing CO enough to trigger reflex increases in contractility, and eventually leading to disuse atrophy of the LV. The absence of volume-overload signs in this patient (no JVD, no edema, no crackles) supports preserved LV mechanics at rest.

distractor_notes.A: Reduced LV contractility implies primary LV systolic dysfunction (e.g. ischemic or dilated cardiomyopathy), not MS where the LV muscle is uninvolved
distractor_notes.B: Increased LV diastolic pressure with normal contractility describes a primary LV diastolic dysfunction (e.g. hypertensive heart disease, amyloidosis); in MS the LV is protected from elevated upstream pressures because the obstruction is at the valve
distractor_notes.C: Reduced LV contractility is not a feature of MS; this fits dilated CMP

stem_paraphrase: A 70-year-old woman comes to the office due to progressive exertional dyspnea over the last year. She has no chest pain or lower extremity swelling. The patient has long-standing hypertension but has not always been adherent to her medical regimen. She is a lifetime nonsmoker. Blood pressure is 164/73 mm Hg and pulse is 88 per minute and regular. An S4 is heard on cardiac auscultation. The lungs are clear. Echocardiogram reveals left atrial enlargement, moderate concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 67%. Which of the following changes in left ventricular compliance, angiotensin II signaling, and cardiac myosin heavy chain-beta expression are most likely to be seen in this patient?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased LV compliance, increased angiotensin II signaling, increased beta-MHC expression
B. Decreased LV compliance, decreased angiotensin II signaling, increased beta-MHC expression
C. Decreased LV compliance, increased angiotensin II signaling, decreased beta-MHC expression
D. Increased LV compliance, increased angiotensin II signaling, increased beta-MHC expression
E. Increased LV compliance, increased angiotensin II signaling, decreased beta-MHC expression
F. Increased LV compliance, decreased angiotensin II signaling, decreased beta-MHC expression
explanation_gist: The clinical picture (chronic uncontrolled hypertension, S4 on auscultation, preserved ejection fraction with concentric LVH on echo) is heart failure with preserved ejection fraction from hypertensive heart disease. Three integrated changes accompany this remodeling. First, chronic pressure overload drives the cardiomyocyte to add new contractile units (sarcomeres) in PARALLEL with existing ones, thickening the wall without enlarging the chamber. The contractile protein composition shifts toward greater expression of beta-myosin heavy chain (the slower, more energy-efficient isoform that predominates in pressure-overloaded ventricles). Second, the thickened wall is less compliant: it resists distension during diastole, raising LVEDP for any given filling volume. The S4 reflects this stiff wall, generated when atrial systole forces blood into a non-compliant LV. Third, the reduced stroke volume and renal perfusion that follow from impaired filling activate the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Angiotensin II signaling rises, producing systemic vasoconstriction, sodium retention via aldosterone, and additional myocyte remodeling. Over time the RAAS activation contributes to further fibrosis and decompensation, completing the maladaptive loop in HFpEF.

distractor_notes.B, C, E, F: These combinations mix changes that are not internally consistent for pressure-overload concentric remodeling, where compliance falls, RAAS activity rises, and contractile protein expression shifts toward the beta isoform together
distractor_notes.D: Increased LV compliance with up-regulated beta-MHC fits volume-overload eccentric hypertrophy (e.g. chronic AR or MR), not pressure-overload concentric LVH

stem_paraphrase: A 33-year-old man comes to the office for fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance over the past 6 months. He was diagnosed with infective endocarditis in the setting of intravenous drug use a year ago and completed a course of antibiotic therapy. He has not used drugs since then. He has no other medical conditions and takes no medications. A cardiac murmur is heard on auscultation. Both carotid arteries demonstrate a rapid rise and fall of the arterial pulse. Partial compression of the femoral arteries by the stethoscope elicits a systolic-diastolic bruit. Which of the following sets of pressure findings is most likely to be observed during cardiac catheterization of this patient (aortic pressure / left ventricular pressure)?
explanation_gist: The bounding rapid rise-fall arterial pulse (Corrigan pulse) and the systolic-diastolic femoral bruit elicited by partial compression (Duroziez sign), arising in a patient with recent infective endocarditis, are characteristic of chronic aortic regurgitation. Endocarditis-induced cusp damage allows blood to flow back into the LV throughout diastole. Four hemodynamic consequences explain the cath numbers. First, aortic diastolic pressure falls steeply because blood continuously runs off backward through the incompetent valve (so aortic pressure 60 here, much lower than the normal 80). Second, the LV receives both normal mitral inflow AND the regurgitant volume in diastole, so LV end-diastolic pressure rises (20 here versus a normal 10). Third, the LV undergoes eccentric hypertrophy over time to accommodate the chronically larger preload, raising stroke volume substantially. Fourth, the high stroke volume ejected into a more compliant aorta produces a high peak aortic systolic pressure (160 here, versus a normal 120). The widened pulse pressure (160 minus 60 = 100) is what produces the bounding peripheral findings. LV systolic pressure equals aortic systolic pressure because the valve still opens normally during ejection (no obstruction to outflow).

distractor_notes.A: LV systolic pressure greater than aortic systolic pressure indicates a pressure gradient across the aortic valve in systole, i.e. aortic stenosis (not regurgitation)
distractor_notes.B: Normal aortic and LV pressures, no abnormality, inconsistent with the exam findings
distractor_notes.C: Normal aortic pressure with mildly elevated LVEDP fits compensated systolic or diastolic dysfunction without volume overload
distractor_notes.E: Hypertensive picture with mildly elevated systolic aortic pressure and normal LVEDP, no regurgitation, fits chronic hypertension

stem_paraphrase: A 55-year-old man is brought to the hospital immediately after a sudden collapse at home 20 minutes ago. The patient reported chest pain and then collapsed to the floor while trying to sit down. Medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Temperature is 36.7 C, blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg, pulse is 120 per minute, and respirations are 22 per minute. On examination the patient is in marked respiratory distress. Bilateral crackles are present. An S3 is audible. ECG reveals ST-segment elevation in leads V2 through V6. Which of the following hemodynamic changes (pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, central venous pressure, coronary perfusion pressure) is most likely present in this patient?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased PCWP, decreased CVP, decreased coronary perfusion pressure
B. Decreased PCWP, increased CVP, decreased coronary perfusion pressure
C. Increased PCWP, decreased CVP, increased coronary perfusion pressure
D. Increased PCWP, increased CVP, decreased coronary perfusion pressure
E. Increased PCWP, increased CVP, increased coronary perfusion pressure
explanation_gist: Anterior wall ST-elevation MI involving leads V2-V6 indicates extensive damage to the LAD territory and the anterior LV. The hypotension, pulmonary crackles, and S3 establish acute LV failure with cardiogenic shock. The hemodynamic profile reflects three failures. First, the infarcted LV cannot eject enough blood forward, so LV end-diastolic pressure rises and transmits retrograde through the left atrium into the pulmonary venous bed, raising PCWP and producing pulmonary congestion (the crackles). Second, the same LV failure backs up further into the right heart over time, raising central venous pressure as right ventricular function deteriorates or as the elevated pulmonary pressures challenge the RV. Third, coronary perfusion pressure equals aortic diastolic pressure minus left ventricular end-diastolic pressure. In this patient, aortic diastolic is low (hypotension) AND LVEDP is high (failing LV), so the gradient driving blood through the coronary circulation in diastole is markedly compressed, lowering coronary perfusion pressure. This sets up a vicious cycle: reduced coronary flow worsens myocardial ischemia, which worsens contractility, which deepens the shock.

distractor_notes.A: Pattern fits hypovolemic or distributive shock (low filling pressures and reduced perfusion)
distractor_notes.B: Decreased PCWP with increased CVP suggests obstructive shock (e.g. pulmonary embolism or tension pneumothorax) where forward flow to the left heart is blocked
distractor_notes.C: Decreased CVP and increased CPP are not consistent with cardiogenic shock
distractor_notes.E: Increased CPP would not be seen with hypotension; CPP requires adequate aortic diastolic pressure

stem_paraphrase: A 2-year-old previously healthy boy is brought to the emergency department for a 4-day history of poor feeding, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. He has had 5 to 7 watery bowel movements per day. Temperature is 36.8 C and blood pressure is 65/42 mm Hg. On physical examination he is lethargic with dry mucous membranes and sunken eyes. Lungs are clear to auscultation. No murmurs are heard. The abdomen is distended and tender with hyperactive bowel sounds. Which of the following changes is most likely present in this patient?
options_paraphrase: A. Extravasation of fluid from systemic capillaries
E. Relaxation of venous smooth muscle
explanation_gist: The combination of prolonged diarrhea, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, hypotension, and lethargy indicates hypovolemic shock from gastrointestinal fluid losses. The downstream physiologic chain proceeds as follows. Hypovolemia reduces cardiac output and tissue perfusion. Tissues respond to hypoperfusion by switching from aerobic to anaerobic metabolism, generating lactic acid. The accumulating lactic acid (along with bicarbonate losses in stool that contribute additional metabolic acidosis) lowers blood pH. Peripheral and central chemoreceptors detect the acidosis and trigger increased ventilatory rate, blowing off CO2 to generate a compensatory respiratory alkalosis that partially restores pH. The increased ventilatory rate is the only listed acute compensatory change. Alongside this, the same low cardiac output triggers baroreflex-mediated sympathetic activation, producing arteriolar vasoconstriction (reducing arteriolar capacitance, the opposite of choice B), venous smooth muscle contraction (the opposite of E), and a fall in CVP because the central venous compartment loses volume (opposite of C). Choice A (capillary extravasation) is not a feature; intravascular fluid is conserved in this state.

distractor_notes.A: Capillary fluid extravasation is a feature of distributive shock (sepsis, anaphylaxis), not hypovolemia where the body actively reabsorbs interstitial fluid into capillaries to expand the intravascular space (reverse Starling)
distractor_notes.B: Arteriolar capacitance decreases due to vasoconstriction in hypovolemic shock, not increases
distractor_notes.C: Central venous pressure drops with volume loss; it would only rise in cardiogenic or obstructive shock
distractor_notes.E: Venous smooth muscle contracts (does not relax) in hypovolemic shock, reducing venous capacitance to maintain venous return

stem_paraphrase: A 66-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up appointment. He has a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and continues to have persistent dyspnea despite taking maximum doses of his heart failure medications. He has had no lightheadedness or chest pain. Blood pressure is 133/72 mm Hg and pulse is 76 per minute. Physical examination reveals normal lung sounds and no lower extremity edema. His medical regimen is modified with the addition of a combination pill that inhibits neprilysin and blocks angiotensin II receptors. Which of the following is the most likely effect of this medication?
explanation_gist: The combination pill described is sacubitril plus valsartan (ARNI therapy), now standard for HFrEF after the PARADIGM-HF trial. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, the metalloprotease that breaks down circulating ANP and BNP (and also angiotensin II, among other peptides). By inhibiting neprilysin, sacubitril prolongs the half-life of these natriuretic peptides. ANP and BNP bind natriuretic peptide receptors on target tissues, activate guanylate cyclase, and raise intracellular cGMP, producing four key effects: dilation of renal afferent arterioles with constriction of efferent arterioles (raising GFR), inhibition of sodium reabsorption in the collecting duct (driving natriuresis and accompanying diuresis), inhibition of renin and aldosterone secretion (counteracting RAAS), and systemic arteriolar vasodilation. The dominant clinical result is increased urinary sodium excretion (and accompanying free water excretion), reducing intravascular volume, ventricular preload, and pulmonary congestion. Because neprilysin also degrades angiotensin II, blocking neprilysin without addressing angiotensin would allow harmful angiotensin II accumulation. The ARB component (valsartan) blocks angiotensin II receptors to prevent this off-target effect.

distractor_notes.A: Free water reabsorption is blocked by V2 receptor antagonists (e.g. tolvaptan) acting on collecting duct aquaporins; ARNI primarily drives sodium and accompanying water excretion, not pure aquaresis
distractor_notes.B: ANP and BNP relax vascular smooth muscle, so venous compliance INCREASES with ARNI (not decreases)
distractor_notes.C: ARNI improves cardiac performance by reducing afterload and volume overload, but it does not directly increase contractility
distractor_notes.D: ARNI causes vasodilation and reduces peripheral arterial resistance, not increases it

stem_paraphrase: A 46-year-old man comes to the office for an annual examination. He has an uncomfortable heartbeat sensation at night that he tries to relieve by sleeping on his right side. The patient has had mild shortness of breath with exertion over the last 6 months but no chest pain. He was told during a wellness check approximately 2 years ago that he had a heart murmur. He has no other medical problems. Physical examination reveals bounding femoral pulses and carotid pulsations accompanied by head bobbing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation_gist: The combination of an uncomfortable awareness of the heartbeat (often perceived worse in left lateral decubitus position, here mitigated by sleeping on the right side), bounding femoral pulses, head bobbing (de Musset sign), and visible carotid pulsations forms the classic constellation of chronic aortic regurgitation. The pathophysiology: incompetent aortic valve leaflets allow blood to flow backward from the aorta into the LV throughout diastole. The LV dilates and undergoes eccentric hypertrophy to accommodate the chronically larger preload, generating an increased stroke volume to compensate. The resulting wide pulse pressure (high systolic from large SV, low diastolic from backward runoff) produces the bounding peripheral findings: Corrigan pulse (visible quick rise and fall of carotid), water-hammer pulse, head bobbing from momentum transfer to the skull, even rhythmic pulsations of the liver, spleen, and fingertips. The blowing early diastolic decrescendo murmur of AR is best heard at the left sternal border in the third or fourth intercostal space.

distractor_notes.B: Aortic stenosis produces pulsus parvus et tardus (small, slow-rising pulses), not bounding pulses, due to reduced and delayed forward stroke volume
distractor_notes.C: ASD causes a left-to-right intracardiac shunt with hyperdynamic right heart impulse but does not alter peripheral pulse character
distractor_notes.D: Coarctation produces upper-extremity hypertension with diminished or delayed femoral pulses (brachial-femoral delay), the opposite of bounding femoral pulses
distractor_notes.E: Mitral regurgitation typically preserves normal peripheral pulses in chronic compensated cases
distractor_notes.F: Mitral stenosis reduces stroke volume and produces small-volume peripheral pulses, not bounding ones
distractor_notes.G: Pulmonary stenosis is a right-sided lesion and does not produce systemic bounding pulses
distractor_notes.H: Tricuspid regurgitation is right-sided and does not affect arterial pulses

stem_paraphrase: A 43-year-old man comes to the emergency department after experiencing 4 episodes of coffee-ground emesis that started earlier this morning. He also describes epigastric pain over the last 3 to 4 months that was relieved by over-the-counter antacids. He has no other past medical history. He has smoked a pack of cigarettes daily for the last 20 years but does not use alcohol or recreational drugs. Blood pressure is 70/40 mm Hg and pulse is 130 per minute and regular. Extremities are cool to the touch with loss of skin turgor. Intravenous access is obtained via 2 large-bore peripheral catheters and rapid infusion of 2 liters of normal saline is initiated. This intervention is most likely to increase which of the following hemodynamic parameters?
explanation_gist: The patient is in hypovolemic shock from an upper GI bleed (coffee-ground emesis from oxidized hemoglobin and the prior history of peptic ulcer disease are characteristic). Acute volume loss leaves the ventricles underfilled and reduces cardiac output. Rapid infusion of crystalloid restores intravascular volume, which immediately raises venous return to the right heart and, through the pulmonary circulation, raises LV preload. The increased filling stretches the LV during diastole, lengthening the cardiomyocyte sarcomeres at end-diastole. This is the direct mechanical link to the Frank-Starling mechanism: longer sarcomere length up to an optimal point increases the number of actin-myosin cross-bridges that can form during the next contraction, producing a larger stroke volume on the next beat. The net effect is increased CO with a single dose of fluid, restoring perfusion pressure. The other listed parameters either do not respond to fluid loading or move in the opposite direction (sympathetic withdrawal after volume restoration reduces HR, TPR, contractility, and renin release).

distractor_notes.A: Aortic wall elasticity is an intrinsic property of the aortic media; it is not altered by fluid administration
distractor_notes.B: Diastolic compliance is determined by myocardial properties (collagen content, hypertrophy, infiltrative disease); it is not affected by crystalloid
distractor_notes.D: Heart rate FALLS once fluid resuscitation lowers sympathetic drive; it does not increase
distractor_notes.E: Plasma renin activity FALLS after volume restoration; it would have been elevated during shock
distractor_notes.F: Total peripheral resistance DROPS as sympathetic vasoconstriction recedes after fluid restoration
distractor_notes.G: Contractility falls as sympathetic activity decreases with restored perfusion

stem_paraphrase: A 44-year-old previously healthy woman comes to the emergency department for a 2-day history of left flank pain, dysuria, fever, and chills. Temperature is 38.9 C, blood pressure is 80/40 mm Hg, and pulse is 140 per minute and regular. Physical examination reveals left costovertebral angle tenderness. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis, pyuria, and bacteriuria. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics and IV fluids are administered. The patient remains persistently hypotensive, and an intravenous phenylephrine infusion is started. Several minutes later her heart rate decreases to 100 per minute. The infusion most likely induced which of the following intracellular changes in this patient (cyclic AMP level in ventricular myocytes, inositol trisphosphate level in vascular smooth muscle, inward calcium current in sinoatrial nodal cells)?
options_paraphrase: A. Decreased cAMP in ventricular myocytes, increased IP3 in vascular SM, decreased inward Ca current in SA nodal cells
B. Decreased cAMP in ventricular myocytes, decreased IP3 in vascular SM, no change in inward Ca current
C. Increased cAMP in ventricular myocytes, increased IP3 in vascular SM, increased inward Ca current in SA nodal cells
D. Increased cAMP in ventricular myocytes, no change in IP3, increased inward Ca current in SA nodal cells
E. No change in cAMP, decreased IP3 in vascular SM, decreased inward Ca current
image_description: A reference table summarizing autonomic receptor signaling: alpha-1 raises IP3 (vasoconstriction, urethral constriction, pupillary dilation), alpha-2 lowers cAMP (CNS sympatholytic), beta-1 raises cAMP (cardiac contractility and HR, renin release), beta-2 raises cAMP (vasodilation, bronchodilation), M1 raises IP3 (intestinal motility), M2 lowers cAMP (decreased cardiac contractility and HR), M3 raises IP3 (bronchoconstriction, increased insulin and motility, bladder and pupillary contraction, peripheral vasodilation via NO).
explanation_gist: Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-1 adrenergic agonist. The direct effect of phenylephrine on vascular smooth muscle is alpha-1 receptor activation, coupled to Gq, which activates phospholipase C and raises inositol trisphosphate (IP3) and diacylglycerol. The rise in IP3 releases sarcoplasmic Ca into the cytoplasm, producing smooth muscle contraction and systemic vasoconstriction. The resulting rise in arterial blood pressure stretches baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch, triggering the baroreceptor reflex. The brainstem responds by increasing parasympathetic outflow via the vagus to the heart and decreasing sympathetic outflow. At the heart, two changes follow: reduced beta-1 stimulation lowers cAMP in ventricular myocytes (reducing contractility and indirectly slowing the heart), and increased M2 muscarinic stimulation at the SA node lowers cAMP and reduces the inward Ca current that drives phase 4 spontaneous depolarization, slowing nodal firing. The net result on the SA node is the bradycardia observed. So all three changes occur simultaneously: ventricular cAMP drops (baroreflex), vascular IP3 rises (direct alpha-1 effect), SA nodal inward Ca current falls (baroreflex).

distractor_notes.B: No change in IP3 ignores the direct alpha-1 effect on vascular smooth muscle that is the entire pharmacologic mechanism of phenylephrine
distractor_notes.C: Both cAMP and inward Ca increased would be the SYMPATHETIC response (e.g. an epinephrine bolus), not the parasympathetic-dominant baroreflex response to the BP rise here
distractor_notes.D: Increased cAMP in cardiac tissue would be expected with sympathetic activation, not the baroreflex response to phenylephrine-induced BP elevation
distractor_notes.E: Decreased IP3 in vascular smooth muscle contradicts the direct alpha-1 mechanism of phenylephrine

stem_paraphrase: A 35-year-old woman comes to the office for a follow-up appointment after recent hospitalization. She presented to the emergency department with fever and malaise, was diagnosed with infective endocarditis, and underwent a prolonged course of antibiotic therapy. Her last dose was 5 days ago. She has no fever or shortness of breath and has resumed her daily activities. Blood pressure is 128/72 mm Hg and pulse is 78 per minute. Auscultatory findings recorded at the left sternal border are shown in the phonocardiogram below. The strip shows a holosystolic murmur during the expiration phase that becomes notably louder and more prolonged during the inspiration phase. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the murmur depicted above?
image_description: A phonocardiogram strip showing two respiratory phases. During expiration: S1, a holosystolic murmur between S1 and S2 of moderate amplitude, then S2. During inspiration: same pattern but the holosystolic murmur is visibly louder and longer. The respiratory variation in murmur intensity is the diagnostic feature.
explanation_gist: Two features narrow the differential: the murmur is holosystolic (filling the entire systole between S1 and S2), and it becomes louder during inspiration. Holosystolic murmurs are produced by three conditions: mitral regurgitation, tricuspid regurgitation, and ventricular septal defect. Among these, only tricuspid regurgitation predictably increases in intensity with inspiration. This is Carvallo sign and reflects respiratory physiology: during inspiration the negative intrathoracic pressure increases venous return to the right heart, raising RV preload and stroke volume, which in turn increases the regurgitant volume crossing the incompetent tricuspid valve per beat. Mitral regurgitation often shows minimal respiratory variation or even slight diminution with inspiration because left ventricular filling actually drops slightly during inspiration (pulmonary venous return is briefly diverted to expand pulmonary capacitance). VSD murmurs also typically show no respiratory variation. TR most often arises from chronically elevated pulmonary pressures (functional TR) or, in this patient with prior IE, from direct valve damage from the infection (a common complication of right-sided endocarditis in IV drug users). It is best heard at the left lower sternal border (third or fourth intercostal space, where this strip was recorded).

distractor_notes.A: Aortic stenosis produces a mid-systolic ejection (crescendo-decrescendo) murmur at the right upper sternal border, not a holosystolic murmur
distractor_notes.B: MR is holosystolic but is best heard at the apex (not LSB) and does not significantly increase with inspiration
distractor_notes.C: MS produces a diastolic murmur with an opening snap, not a holosystolic murmur
distractor_notes.D: Pulmonic regurgitation creates an early diastolic murmur (not holosystolic), starting at S2

stem_paraphrase: Researchers are studying the coronary circulation in various healthy and diseased states using an experimental animal whose cardiovascular physiology is similar to that of humans. They measure blood flow and perfusion pressure in one of the epicardial coronary arteries (baseline measurement shown as a blue circle on a coronary blood flow versus perfusion pressure plot). They then clamp the artery to simulate 70% atherosclerotic stenosis and re-measure the blood flow and perfusion pressure distal to the lesion. Which of the following sets of changes is most likely observed once a steady state is achieved?
options_paraphrase: A. Increased perfusion pressure with increased blood flow distal to stenosis
B. Slight increase in blood flow at unchanged perfusion pressure
C. Decreased blood flow at unchanged perfusion pressure
D. Decreased perfusion pressure distal to stenosis with blood flow restored to near-baseline by autoregulation
E. Decreased perfusion pressure with collapsed blood flow
image_description: Coronary flow versus perfusion pressure plot. A blue circle marks the baseline operating point. Four candidate post-stenosis points are scattered: A (higher flow at similar pressure), B (very slightly higher flow at slightly lower pressure), C (similar pressure but lower flow), and D (substantially lower perfusion pressure but flow restored to a level near baseline through arteriolar dilation).
explanation_gist: Coronary autoregulation maintains relatively constant myocardial blood flow across a wide range of perfusion pressures (approximately 60 to 160 mm Hg). The mechanism: the resistance vessels of the coronary circulation (the arterioles, downstream of the epicardial arteries) tone up or down in response to changes in distal perfusion pressure or local metabolic demand. When a significant epicardial stenosis (e.g. 70%) is introduced, perfusion pressure distal to the lesion immediately drops because of the resistance the stenosis adds. The drop in distal flow initially reduces oxygen delivery to the myocardium downstream, creating local ischemia and accumulation of vasodilator metabolites: adenosine (from ATP breakdown when oxygen falls below demand), nitric oxide (from endothelial production in response to shear stress and metabolic signals), and other mediators. These vasodilators relax the downstream arterioles, lowering arteriolar resistance and partially restoring distal blood flow despite the persistently low perfusion pressure. The new steady state therefore shows lower perfusion pressure distal to the stenosis with blood flow nearly restored to baseline (point D). The autoregulatory reserve is exhausted once arterioles reach maximal dilation; beyond that, further decreases in perfusion pressure lead to a steep drop in flow and produce angina or ischemia.

distractor_notes.A, B, C: All of these scenarios fail to account for the drop in perfusion pressure that necessarily occurs distal to a stenosis (point B is close but understates the pressure drop and the autoregulatory recovery)
distractor_notes.E: Total collapse of flow occurs only when autoregulatory reserve is exhausted (extreme stenosis or very low aortic pressure); a 70% stenosis at normal aortic pressure remains within autoregulation range

stem_paraphrase: A 63-year-old man comes to the emergency department for dyspnea. Over the past several days he has experienced progressively worsening shortness of breath while walking his dog around the block. He could not breathe while lying in bed last night and fell asleep only after moving to a recliner. He had an acute myocardial infarction 2 years ago and has been nonadherent with his medications and follow-up appointments. Temperature is 36.7 C, blood pressure is 122/74 mm Hg, pulse is 94 per minute, and respirations are 22 per minute. Physical examination shows bibasilar lung crackles, jugular venous distension, and bilateral pitting edema in the lower extremities. Chest x-ray reveals cardiomegaly and pulmonary venous congestion. Which of the following factors is most likely contributing to this patient's symptoms?
options_paraphrase: D. Increased plasma brain natriuretic peptide concentration
E. Increased sympathetic nervous system activity
explanation_gist: The patient has acute decompensation of chronic heart failure (post-MI HFrEF with ongoing maladaptive remodeling and now overt congestion). Heart failure is fundamentally a neurohormonal disease: an initial drop in cardiac output triggers compensatory activation of the sympathetic nervous system and the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, both intended to maintain perfusion in the short term but ultimately accelerating cardiac deterioration over time. Increased sympathetic outflow does several things acutely: it raises heart rate and contractility (positive chronotropy and inotropy), constricts peripheral arterioles to maintain blood pressure, and constricts venous capacitance vessels to push more blood centrally (raising preload). Over months and years, however, sustained sympathetic drive becomes deleterious: chronic catecholamine exposure causes myocyte hypertrophy and apoptosis, fibrosis, downregulation of beta-receptors, and progressive cavity remodeling. The peripheral vasoconstriction also raises afterload, increasing the workload on an already failing ventricle. This is why beta-blockers (which oppose chronic sympathetic activation) have a mortality benefit in HFrEF despite their initial negative inotropic effect. The same logic explains why ACEI/ARB/MRA and ARNI all target the RAAS arm. So among the listed options, the increased SNS activity is the dominant maladaptive driver of this patient's symptoms.

distractor_notes.A: Arteriolar resistance INCREASES in HF (due to sympathetic and RAAS activation), not decreases
distractor_notes.B: Plasma renin activity INCREASES in HF as the kidneys sense reduced perfusion; this is part of RAAS activation, not opposite
distractor_notes.C: Ventricular end-diastolic pressures are ELEVATED in decompensated HF (this is the source of pulmonary and systemic congestion), not decreased
distractor_notes.D: BNP does rise as a counter-regulatory response to myocardial stretch, but it has BENEFICIAL effects (vasodilation, natriuresis, opposing RAAS); it does not contribute to symptoms

stem_paraphrase: A 45-year-old man comes to the emergency department with a 2-day history of dyspnea, orthopnea, and ankle swelling. This morning he also developed nausea, vomiting, and a headache. Blood pressure is elevated, and physical examination reveals pulmonary crackles and bilateral pitting edema to the knees. A sodium nitroprusside infusion is started. Which of the following left ventricular pressure-volume loop changes best represents the effect of this therapy? Solid loop = pre-therapy, dashed loop = during infusion.
options_paraphrase: A. Unchanged preload with increased stroke volume from a steeper end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (positive inotrope pattern)
B. Increased preload with increased stroke volume and modest rise in peak pressure (Frank-Starling response)
C. Increased preload and afterload with unchanged contractility (pure arterial and venous vasoconstrictor)
D. Decreased preload with mildly decreased stroke volume and modest reduction in afterload (predominantly venous vasodilator)
E. Decreased preload and afterload with stroke volume preserved (balanced arterial and venous vasodilator)
image_description: Five LV pressure-volume loop options, each showing a solid baseline loop with a dashed post-therapy loop overlaid. Option E shows the dashed loop shifted leftward at end-diastole (lower preload, smaller EDV), with a modest reduction in peak systolic pressure (lower afterload), and a similar width between end-diastolic and end-systolic points (preserved stroke volume despite the smaller volumes overall).
explanation_gist: Sodium nitroprusside is a short-acting, potent vasodilator that releases nitric oxide spontaneously in the bloodstream, dilating both arteries and veins simultaneously (balanced vasodilator effect). In acute hypertensive heart failure, this combined effect produces three changes on the LV pressure-volume loop. First, venous dilation increases venous capacitance, reducing venous return and lowering LV preload; on the loop this shifts the end-diastolic point leftward to a lower EDV. Second, arterial dilation reduces systemic vascular resistance, lowering the afterload against which the LV must eject; on the loop this lowers the peak systolic pressure during ejection. Third, contractility (the slope of the end-systolic pressure-volume relationship) is unchanged. The net result is that stroke volume is preserved at a much lower LV pressure, dramatically reducing the LV pressure-volume area (work per beat) and the myocardial oxygen demand. This is exactly what is needed in acute decompensated heart failure with hypertension: reduced congestive symptoms (from lowered preload) and a less strained ventricle (from lowered afterload), without sacrificing cardiac output. The balanced vasodilator profile distinguishes nitroprusside from primarily venous dilators like nitroglycerin (which mostly reduce preload) and primarily arterial dilators like hydralazine (which mostly reduce afterload).

distractor_notes.A: Unchanged preload with steeper ESPVR is the pattern of a positive inotrope (digoxin, dobutamine), not nitroprusside
distractor_notes.B: Increased preload and stroke volume describes a Frank-Starling response to volume loading, not pharmacologic vasodilation
distractor_notes.C: Increased preload and afterload with unchanged contractility is the pattern of a pure vasoconstrictor (phenylephrine), the opposite of nitroprusside
distractor_notes.D: Mildly reduced preload with little afterload change describes pure venous dilators (nitroglycerin, isosorbide mononitrate), not nitroprusside

stem_paraphrase: A 65-year-old man reports multiple episodes of lightheadedness while buttoning a tight shirt collar. During 2 episodes he passed out briefly but sustained no injuries. His blood pressure was 70/40 mm Hg and pulse was 45 per minute during one of the episodes. Past medical history is significant for hypertension and diet-controlled diabetes mellitus. He is a lifetime nonsmoker and drinks alcohol on social occasions. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 125/72 mm Hg and pulse is 76 per minute without orthostatic changes. Stimulation of afferent sensory fibers in which of the following nerves is most likely responsible for this patient's symptoms?
explanation_gist: The patient has carotid sinus hypersensitivity. Mechanical pressure from a tight shirt collar transiently compresses and deforms the carotid sinus (a dilated segment of the proximal internal carotid artery just above the carotid bifurcation). The carotid sinus contains stretch-sensitive baroreceptors that fire when arterial wall tension rises. In carotid sinus hypersensitivity these baroreceptors over-respond to compression, generating an exaggerated afferent signal that triggers a profound vagally-mediated reflex: increased parasympathetic outflow to the heart causes bradycardia and reduced AV conduction, and reduced sympathetic outflow causes vasodilation and reduced peripheral resistance. The combined hypotension and bradycardia drops cerebral perfusion and causes syncope. The carotid sinus afferent pathway runs through the Hering nerve, which joins the glossopharyngeal nerve (cranial nerve IX) and projects to the nucleus tractus solitarius in the medulla. The efferent limb returns via the vagus nerve (CN X) to the heart and peripheral vasculature. So the AFFERENT side of the carotid sinus reflex is glossopharyngeal.

distractor_notes.A: Accessory nerve (CN XI) innervates sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, has no role in baroreceptor signaling
distractor_notes.C: Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) controls tongue motor function, no autonomic role
distractor_notes.D: Trigeminal nerve (CN V) carries facial sensation and motor to mastication muscles, no baroreceptor function
distractor_notes.E: The vagus nerve carries the AFFERENT signal from the AORTIC ARCH baroreceptors (different from the carotid sinus) and the EFFERENT limb of the carotid sinus reflex; for the carotid sinus afferent, the answer is glossopharyngeal

stem_paraphrase: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after passing out. The patient was standing behind the dugout watching his friends play baseball when he suddenly felt warm and lightheaded. Then he remembers awakening on the ground. Bystanders reported that the patient was unresponsive for approximately 2 minutes. He feels fatigued but does not have chest pain or shortness of breath. He has no significant medical history and does not use alcohol or recreational drugs. Temperature is 36.7 C, supine blood pressure is 110/65 mm Hg, pulse is 84 per minute, and respirations are 16 per minute. Pulse oximetry is 100% on room air. The patient is awake and alert. Pupils are equal and reactive. Heart and lung examinations are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Neurologic examination is normal. ECG is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's syncope?
options_paraphrase: A. Increased automaticity of the Purkinje fibers (ventricular tachycardia)
B. Increased parasympathetic output to the heart (cardioinhibitory vasovagal response)
C. Increased sympathetic output to the small vessels
D. Reentry arrhythmia using the atrioventricular node (AVNRT)
E. Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve (HCM)
explanation_gist: The clinical picture (young patient, prolonged standing in warm weather, classic prodrome of warmth and lightheadedness, brief loss of consciousness with rapid recovery, normal exam and ECG afterward) is characteristic of vasovagal syncope, a form of reflex syncope. The mechanism involves aberrant autonomic reflex output triggered by various stimuli: prolonged standing, emotional stress, pain, heat, dehydration, and visual triggers (blood, needles) can all activate it. Once triggered, the brainstem cardiovascular center generates a combined effector response. The cardioinhibitory limb is increased parasympathetic outflow via the vagus nerve to the SA and AV nodes, producing profound bradycardia and reduced contractility. The vasodepressor limb is decreased sympathetic outflow to the systemic vasculature, producing vasodilation of venous capacitance vessels (reducing preload) and peripheral arterioles (reducing afterload). Both effects together drop cardiac output sharply, cerebral perfusion falls, and consciousness is briefly lost. Once the patient becomes recumbent, gravity is no longer pulling blood pool away from the brain, the reflex resolves, and the patient awakens rapidly. The dominant change responsible for the bradycardia and hypotension is the parasympathetic surge to the heart.

distractor_notes.A: Purkinje automaticity drives ventricular tachycardia, usually arising in patients with ischemic or structural heart disease (not normal adolescents); VT is also less likely to produce a vagal prodrome and quick spontaneous recovery
distractor_notes.C: INCREASED sympathetic output to small vessels would CAUSE vasoconstriction and RAISE blood pressure, the opposite of what is needed to produce syncope; the vasodepressor limb of vasovagal syncope is DECREASED sympathetic output, not increased
distractor_notes.D: AVNRT causes a regular narrow-complex tachycardia, often without syncope; if syncope did occur it would be without the warmth/lightheadedness prodrome typical of vasovagal
distractor_notes.E: SAM of the mitral valve is a feature of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, but exertional syncope from outflow obstruction would not show this classic vasovagal prodrome and the patient has a normal cardiac exam and ECG
